EVENT_NARRATIVE: A core of a supercell thunderstorm moved over this location creating strong, damaging microburst winds. The strongest rotational core moved south of this location with eye witnesses reporting a funnel cloud moving over nearby Cayuga Lake. The most intense damaged observed was to a cattle barn, where the roof and wall structure was lofted approximately 250 feet eastward; maximum wind speeds were estimated here at 80 mph. Surrounding damage included numerous large tree branches snapped and few uprooted trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of TS Beryl combined with deep tropical moisture and a stationary front draped across the region produced multiple rounds of heavy showers and storms. This led to numerous reports of flash flooding, some locally severe across portions of eastern New York, mainly in Essex County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dept. of Highways and Emergency Management reported several roads damaged and/or washed out in and around the Elizabethtown, NY area due to 3 to 5 inches of excessive rainfall. These included, but were not limited to Lincoln Pond Rd. and Route 9N between US 9 and Old Military Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad positively tilted upper-level trough approached from the west along with associated surface cold front and pre-frontal trough. The pre-frontal trough moved into an area with high instability (1750-2000 J/kg of MLCAPE). This combined with Effect Bulk Shear values around 25 kt allowed for multiple severe thunderstorms to develop. The storms were able to produce damaging wind gusts across southeast NY, with DCAPE values around 750-1000 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires down on US 9W southbound between Gurnee Avenue and Short Clove Road.

A cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms developed as a weak front crossed the eastern half of the Buffalo forecast area. Instability values of around 1,000 J/kg of CAPE and favorable shear were in place to help develop the storms. Forcing from the front wasn't very strong, so severe thunderstorm coverage was minimal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree downed in the Town of Amboy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough and cold front crossing western and north central New York caused a few rounds of thunderstorms through the day on July 24, 2024. The strongest storms, reaching severe criteria, occurred with the trough as it tracked east with increased instability and shear ahead of it. While most of the thunderstorms remained below severe criteria, a few damage reports were received, including a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees downed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS aerial storm survey found broken hardwood tree damage and uprooted trees in a convergent damage pattern along an upper tributary of the Pennock Brook. Preliminary rating of EF-0 with maximum winds of 85 mph, length of 0.2 miles, and width of 75 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 10, 2024, most of western and central New York were impacted by the remnants of tropical cyclone Beryl. The event brought every type of warm season severe hazard, including 10 tornadoes across the entire state, numerous reports of wind damage, hail and significant flash flooding. ||As the remnants of Beryl approached the region, a warm front out ahead of/associated with the system provided one of the main focus points for the severe weather. The front, draped from west to east across the state, generally along/near I90 remained nearly stationary through the day, moving only slowly north. Instability increased greatly along and south of the front. With the system being of tropical origin, it was highly dynamic with strong shear, especially along and just north of the warm front across the area. A passing mid-level trough during the event also helped to increase the severe potential for the day. ||The event resulted in numerous tornado warnings issued across western and central New York with 10 tornadoes confirmed for the day. Wind damage from thunderstorms was also widespread across the area, mainly from near Lake Erie, northeast to Rochester and the northern Finger Lakes, to the north country. Other than the tornadoes during this event, the most significant issue was flash flooding that occurred in Lewis County. ||Significant flash flooding occurred in the Town of Lowville, where roads washed out, water rescues were required and numerous buildings were flooded, including the Lowville Academy and Central School. Rainfall amounts in Lowville were nearly 6.50 inches. Flood damage in Lowville totaled $50 million.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree downed on a generator.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level trough and mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) crossed western and central New York during the afternoon on July 15, 2024. Timing with the MCV corresponded with favorable daytime heating and partly to mostly sunny skies ahead of the system. This resulted in more than 2,000 J/kg of CAPE across much of the region south & southeast of Lake Ontario. Combined with the dynamic nature and the shear associated with the MCV itself, severe thunderstorms developed and moved across the area. ||For the Buffalo forecast area, most of the damage was confined to western New York and the northern Finger Lakes. The thunderstorms resulted in two confirmed tornadoes, flash flooding, tennis ball sized hail (2.5) and numerous trees and wires downed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree downed, blocking road.

A pre-frontal trough and cold front crossing western and north central New York caused a few rounds of thunderstorms through the day on July 24, 2024. The strongest storms, reaching severe criteria, occurred with the trough as it tracked east with increased instability and shear ahead of it. While most of the thunderstorms remained below severe criteria, a few damage reports were received, including a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees downed along Chautauqua Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 10, 2024, most of western and central New York were impacted by the remnants of tropical cyclone Beryl. The event brought every type of warm season severe hazard, including 10 tornadoes across the entire state, numerous reports of wind damage, hail and significant flash flooding. ||As the remnants of Beryl approached the region, a warm front out ahead of/associated with the system provided one of the main focus points for the severe weather. The front, draped from west to east across the state, generally along/near I90 remained nearly stationary through the day, moving only slowly north. Instability increased greatly along and south of the front. With the system being of tropical origin, it was highly dynamic with strong shear, especially along and just north of the warm front across the area. A passing mid-level trough during the event also helped to increase the severe potential for the day. ||The event resulted in numerous tornado warnings issued across western and central New York with 10 tornadoes confirmed for the day. Wind damage from thunderstorms was also widespread across the area, mainly from near Lake Erie, northeast to Rochester and the northern Finger Lakes, to the north country. Other than the tornadoes during this event, the most significant issue was flash flooding that occurred in Lewis County. ||Significant flash flooding occurred in the Town of Lowville, where roads washed out, water rescues were required and numerous buildings were flooded, including the Lowville Academy and Central School. Rainfall amounts in Lowville were nearly 6.50 inches. Flood damage in Lowville totaled $50 million.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees downed in the road off of Bloomingdale Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level trough and mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) crossed western and central New York during the afternoon on July 15, 2024. Timing with the MCV corresponded with favorable daytime heating and partly to mostly sunny skies ahead of the system. This resulted in more than 2,000 J/kg of CAPE across much of the region south & southeast of Lake Ontario. Combined with the dynamic nature and the shear associated with the MCV itself, severe thunderstorms developed and moved across the area. ||For the Buffalo forecast area, most of the damage was confined to western New York and the northern Finger Lakes. The thunderstorms resulted in two confirmed tornadoes, flash flooding, tennis ball sized hail (2.5) and numerous trees and wires downed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree downed, blocking roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strengthening southerly winds brought a moderate to high risk for rip current development along NYC ocean beaches with a combo of 3 to 4 foot southerly wind wave and 2 foot easterly swells. In addition, hot and humid conditions had large crowds flocking to area beaches. Two rip current fatalities occurred in these conditions after hours on July 5th at Coney Island Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Aisha Mohammed, 18, and Zainab Mohammed, 17, of the Bronx were reported missing around 8:10 p.m. Friday near Stillwell Avenue in Brooklyn by a bystander who said they entered the water and disappeared from view. The teens were found on the shoreline at 9:15 p.m. and rushed to Coney Island Hospital, where they were pronounced dead according to the NYPD. ||A relative said it was only their second time going to the beach. They were hanging out with a group of about 12 other people enjoying a sunny day at the popular beach when the skies suddenly opened up in a heavy downpour. A family member said he was returning to the group when he spotted the girls being swept out to sea in a strong current.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well-defined shortwave trough and embedded MCV from the previous night's severe MCS tracked towards the region. Strong heating allowed for the area to destabilize, with MLCAPE values around 1000-2000 J/kg. This combined with 30 kt of Effective Bulk Shear allowed for thunderstorms to become severe and produce damaging wind gusts across southeast NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires down across Newport Bridge in Warwick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well-defined shortwave trough and embedded MCV from the previous night's severe MCS tracked towards the region. Strong heating allowed for the area to destabilize, with MLCAPE values around 1000-2000 J/kg. This combined with 30 kt of Effective Bulk Shear allowed for thunderstorms to become severe and produce damaging wind gusts across southeast NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed tree on west Olive Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 10, 2024, most of western and central New York were impacted by the remnants of tropical cyclone Beryl. The event brought every type of warm season severe hazard, including 10 tornadoes across the entire state, numerous reports of wind damage, hail and significant flash flooding. ||As the remnants of Beryl approached the region, a warm front out ahead of/associated with the system provided one of the main focus points for the severe weather. The front, draped from west to east across the state, generally along/near I90 remained nearly stationary through the day, moving only slowly north. Instability increased greatly along and south of the front. With the system being of tropical origin, it was highly dynamic with strong shear, especially along and just north of the warm front across the area. A passing mid-level trough during the event also helped to increase the severe potential for the day. ||The event resulted in numerous tornado warnings issued across western and central New York with 10 tornadoes confirmed for the day. Wind damage from thunderstorms was also widespread across the area, mainly from near Lake Erie, northeast to Rochester and the northern Finger Lakes, to the north country. Other than the tornadoes during this event, the most significant issue was flash flooding that occurred in Lewis County. ||Significant flash flooding occurred in the Town of Lowville, where roads washed out, water rescues were required and numerous buildings were flooded, including the Lowville Academy and Central School. Rainfall amounts in Lowville were nearly 6.50 inches. Flood damage in Lowville totaled $50 million.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and powerlines downed in Sackets Harbor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second day in a row, a mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) crossed western and central New York during the afternoon on July 16, 2024. Much like the day before, partly to mostly sunny skies ahead of the system increased instability across portions of the Buffalo forecast area. The primary difference with the MCV that occurred on this day, was the timing of the track of the system. The MCV moved through similar areas as the day before, but it was around four hours earlier in the day. This resulted in less instability over the western New York area, compared to the previous day. ||While there was some wind damage with this event across portions of western New York, the extent of the damage was much less than the previous day for these areas. Most of the damage with this event occurred from Rochester and points east, but the vast majority of the damage was in central and north central New York. A downburst in Jefferson County caused the most damage with maximum estimated winds to 80 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads in the Town of Lowville flooded, including East State Street, Hemlock Drive, and North State Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak mid-level trough moved across a marginally unstable airmass across northern NY during the late afternoon and evening hours of July 24th. Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and continued into the evening hours with just a few isolated pulse-type storms that produced severe, damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees downed by thunderstorm winds on Judson Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak mid-level trough moved across a marginally unstable airmass across northern NY during the late afternoon and evening hours of July 24th. Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and continued into the evening hours with just a few isolated pulse-type storms that produced severe, damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few reports of trees down and on power lines blocking Barnaby road near West Church street and on Academy road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of Hurricane Beryl tracked across the St. Lawrence Valley of NY and Canada during the afternoon and evening hours of July 10th. Numerous individual supercell-like thunderstorms developed and moved across NY into VT with various wind damage, hail and heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail estimated at 1.5 inches in diameter while hiking between Dail and Nippletop mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 10, 2024, most of western and central New York were impacted by the remnants of tropical cyclone Beryl. The event brought every type of warm season severe hazard, including 10 tornadoes across the entire state, numerous reports of wind damage, hail and significant flash flooding. ||As the remnants of Beryl approached the region, a warm front out ahead of/associated with the system provided one of the main focus points for the severe weather. The front, draped from west to east across the state, generally along/near I90 remained nearly stationary through the day, moving only slowly north. Instability increased greatly along and south of the front. With the system being of tropical origin, it was highly dynamic with strong shear, especially along and just north of the warm front across the area. A passing mid-level trough during the event also helped to increase the severe potential for the day. ||The event resulted in numerous tornado warnings issued across western and central New York with 10 tornadoes confirmed for the day. Wind damage from thunderstorms was also widespread across the area, mainly from near Lake Erie, northeast to Rochester and the northern Finger Lakes, to the north country. Other than the tornadoes during this event, the most significant issue was flash flooding that occurred in Lewis County. ||Significant flash flooding occurred in the Town of Lowville, where roads washed out, water rescues were required and numerous buildings were flooded, including the Lowville Academy and Central School. Rainfall amounts in Lowville were nearly 6.50 inches. Flood damage in Lowville totaled $50 million.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and powerlines downed in Adams Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level trough and mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) crossed western and central New York during the afternoon on July 15, 2024. Timing with the MCV corresponded with favorable daytime heating and partly to mostly sunny skies ahead of the system. This resulted in more than 2,000 J/kg of CAPE across much of the region south & southeast of Lake Ontario. Combined with the dynamic nature and the shear associated with the MCV itself, severe thunderstorms developed and moved across the area. ||For the Buffalo forecast area, most of the damage was confined to western New York and the northern Finger Lakes. The thunderstorms resulted in two confirmed tornadoes, flash flooding, tennis ball sized hail (2.5) and numerous trees and wires downed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree downed at intersection of West Avenue and Bird Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad positively tilted upper-level trough approached from the west along with associated surface cold front and pre-frontal trough. The pre-frontal trough moved into an area with high instability (1750-2000 J/kg of MLCAPE). This combined with Effect Bulk Shear values around 25 kt allowed for multiple severe thunderstorms to develop. The storms were able to produce damaging wind gusts across southeast NY, with DCAPE values around 750-1000 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees down on highway 100 north of Chappaqua Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 10, 2024, most of western and central New York were impacted by the remnants of tropical cyclone Beryl. The event brought every type of warm season severe hazard, including 10 tornadoes across the entire state, numerous reports of wind damage, hail and significant flash flooding. ||As the remnants of Beryl approached the region, a warm front out ahead of/associated with the system provided one of the main focus points for the severe weather. The front, draped from west to east across the state, generally along/near I90 remained nearly stationary through the day, moving only slowly north. Instability increased greatly along and south of the front. With the system being of tropical origin, it was highly dynamic with strong shear, especially along and just north of the warm front across the area. A passing mid-level trough during the event also helped to increase the severe potential for the day. ||The event resulted in numerous tornado warnings issued across western and central New York with 10 tornadoes confirmed for the day. Wind damage from thunderstorms was also widespread across the area, mainly from near Lake Erie, northeast to Rochester and the northern Finger Lakes, to the north country. Other than the tornadoes during this event, the most significant issue was flash flooding that occurred in Lewis County. ||Significant flash flooding occurred in the Town of Lowville, where roads washed out, water rescues were required and numerous buildings were flooded, including the Lowville Academy and Central School. Rainfall amounts in Lowville were nearly 6.50 inches. Flood damage in Lowville totaled $50 million.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters report trees downed and barn roof blown off near Straight Road and Zahm Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level trough and mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) crossed western and central New York during the afternoon on July 15, 2024. Timing with the MCV corresponded with favorable daytime heating and partly to mostly sunny skies ahead of the system. This resulted in more than 2,000 J/kg of CAPE across much of the region south & southeast of Lake Ontario. Combined with the dynamic nature and the shear associated with the MCV itself, severe thunderstorms developed and moved across the area. ||For the Buffalo forecast area, most of the damage was confined to western New York and the northern Finger Lakes. The thunderstorms resulted in two confirmed tornadoes, flash flooding, tennis ball sized hail (2.5) and numerous trees and wires downed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and wires downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad positively tilted upper-level trough approached from the west along with associated surface cold front and pre-frontal trough. The pre-frontal trough moved into an area with high instability (1750-2000 J/kg of MLCAPE). This combined with Effect Bulk Shear values around 25 kt allowed for multiple severe thunderstorms to develop. The storms were able to produce damaging wind gusts across southeast NY, with DCAPE values around 750-1000 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down off NY 22 and Upland Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level trough tracking across the area, along with plenty of instability of 2,000 J/kg or more of CAPE resulted in the formation of thunderstorms across the region. As storms tracked northeast across the Finger Lakes area during the afternoon, a storm produced damage in Geneva. Shear with this setup was limited, resulting in less severe thunderstorms than would have otherwise been possible. Even though there were a few rounds of convection, nearly all of the thunderstorms remained below severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of trees and wires downed in Geneva.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate instability under an upper level area of low pressure across northern VT during the afternoon of July 30th led to isolated thunderstorms with one damage report in Moriah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down along a few roads, including Moriah Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level trough and mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) crossed western and central New York during the afternoon on July 15, 2024. Timing with the MCV corresponded with favorable daytime heating and partly to mostly sunny skies ahead of the system. This resulted in more than 2,000 J/kg of CAPE across much of the region south & southeast of Lake Ontario. Combined with the dynamic nature and the shear associated with the MCV itself, severe thunderstorms developed and moved across the area. ||For the Buffalo forecast area, most of the damage was confined to western New York and the northern Finger Lakes. The thunderstorms resulted in two confirmed tornadoes, flash flooding, tennis ball sized hail (2.5) and numerous trees and wires downed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree downed due to thunderstorms winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 10, 2024, most of western and central New York were impacted by the remnants of tropical cyclone Beryl. The event brought every type of warm season severe hazard, including 10 tornadoes across the entire state, numerous reports of wind damage, hail and significant flash flooding. ||As the remnants of Beryl approached the region, a warm front out ahead of/associated with the system provided one of the main focus points for the severe weather. The front, draped from west to east across the state, generally along/near I90 remained nearly stationary through the day, moving only slowly north. Instability increased greatly along and south of the front. With the system being of tropical origin, it was highly dynamic with strong shear, especially along and just north of the warm front across the area. A passing mid-level trough during the event also helped to increase the severe potential for the day. ||The event resulted in numerous tornado warnings issued across western and central New York with 10 tornadoes confirmed for the day. Wind damage from thunderstorms was also widespread across the area, mainly from near Lake Erie, northeast to Rochester and the northern Finger Lakes, to the north country. Other than the tornadoes during this event, the most significant issue was flash flooding that occurred in Lewis County. ||Significant flash flooding occurred in the Town of Lowville, where roads washed out, water rescues were required and numerous buildings were flooded, including the Lowville Academy and Central School. Rainfall amounts in Lowville were nearly 6.50 inches. Flood damage in Lowville totaled $50 million.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree downed on house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second day in a row, a mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) crossed western and central New York during the afternoon on July 16, 2024. Much like the day before, partly to mostly sunny skies ahead of the system increased instability across portions of the Buffalo forecast area. The primary difference with the MCV that occurred on this day, was the timing of the track of the system. The MCV moved through similar areas as the day before, but it was around four hours earlier in the day. This resulted in less instability over the western New York area, compared to the previous day. ||While there was some wind damage with this event across portions of western New York, the extent of the damage was much less than the previous day for these areas. Most of the damage with this event occurred from Rochester and points east, but the vast majority of the damage was in central and north central New York. A downburst in Jefferson County caused the most damage with maximum estimated winds to 80 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree on top of power lines at the intersection of Baird and Wheelock roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A west to east oriented front along with a passing shortwave trough provided the energy and forcing for a severe thunderstorm to develop over Erie County. The storm was short lived and weakened as it tracked southeast into neighboring Wyoming County. One report was received for each severe hail and thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree downed on Centerline Road near Blakeley Corners Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low pressure system was the driving force of scattered storms across Central New York. Warm and moist air contributed to several rounds of heavy rain producing thunderstorms over the Central Southern Tier. Rainfall amounts in a short period of time totalled between 2 and 3 inches leading to localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Babcock Hollow Rd. flooded out along Stocking Creek near E. Union Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary supported scattered thunderstorms across Central New York. Ahead of the front, an unstable environment supported a few severe thunderstorms. Damaging wind gusts were the main hazard. There were multiple reports of trees and wires down across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree and wires down onto a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed within a highly unstable atmosphere across most of western New York. Heavy rainfall within a very moist atmosphere caused some flooding issues as well during the morning of June 18.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Similar to the day before, strong to severe thunderstorms developed along a frontal boundary draped across the forecast area, south of Lake Ontario. Storms also developed along and east of the Lake Erie lake breeze, and then tracked east-northeast to the Northern Finger Lakes and to Oswego County. Areas south of the frontal boundary had plentiful surface based CAPE values of over 2,000 J/kg, allowing the storms to develop along the different boundaries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down blocking the road near the intersection of Outlet Road and Curran Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system (MCS) and an upper-level wave moved through the region triggering widespread showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours. These features moved into a high instability environment with sufficient shear in place and plenty of moisture, resulting in a ripe environment for a severe weather outbreak. Damaging wind gusts were the main concern though some storms also had tornadoes. Widespread tree and wire damage occurred across Central New York with two microbursts and two tornadoes surveyed making it one of the most active days of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked power lines down which resulted in a brush fire.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms passed through the area with scattered damage on July 15. A focused microburst descended upon the northeast side of Montour Falls, heavily damaging or in many cases uprooting a grove of large trees up to 2 feet in diameter including sycamores. They fell in a divergent pattern, indicating a downburst. Many trees or large branches dropped upon several mobile homes, entrapping 3 residents, while also crushing 2 vehicles and a riding mower. On the front side of the microburst, a Department of Public Works storage building was completely blown apart with |only one wall left standing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second of a multi-day stretch of hot and humid conditions continued on June 19, 2024 across much of eastern New York outside of the Adirondacks and eastern Catskills. Heat index (or feels like temperatures) reached between 95 and 104 degrees across the region.||The heat and humidity also resulted in isolated to widely scattered showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon hours. A few storms became severe downing trees and wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A convectively induced shortwave trough across southern Ontario province and eastern Lake Erie tracked across the forecast area. The trough, along with increased instability, caused strong to severe thunderstorms to develop east of the Lake Erie lake breeze. Severe Thunderstorm Warnings, along with reports of related damage were confined to portions of the Western Southern Tier, Upper Genesee Valley and the Northern Finger Lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees downed on wires in Arcade.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system (MCS) and an upper-level wave moved through the region triggering widespread showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours. These features moved into a high instability environment with sufficient shear in place and plenty of moisture, resulting in a ripe environment for a severe weather outbreak. Damaging wind gusts were the main concern though some storms also had tornadoes. Widespread tree and wire damage occurred across Central New York with two microbursts and two tornadoes surveyed making it one of the most active days of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds snapped multiple trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary supported scattered thunderstorms across Central New York. Ahead of the front, an unstable environment triggered rounds of repeating, heavy rain producing thunderstorms that moved over the same areas. Rainfall totals of 2-3 inches fell in less than 3 hours which contributed to localized flash and urban flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was occurring on Stokes-Lee Center Road threatening a bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A convectively induced shortwave trough across southern Ontario province and eastern Lake Erie tracked across the forecast area. The trough, along with increased instability, caused strong to severe thunderstorms to develop east of the Lake Erie lake breeze. Severe Thunderstorm Warnings, along with reports of related damage were confined to portions of the Western Southern Tier, Upper Genesee Valley and the Northern Finger Lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires downed on County Road 49.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Similar to the day before, strong to severe thunderstorms developed along a frontal boundary draped across the forecast area, south of Lake Ontario. Storms also developed along and east of the Lake Erie lake breeze, and then tracked east-northeast to the Northern Finger Lakes and to Oswego County. Areas south of the frontal boundary had plentiful surface based CAPE values of over 2,000 J/kg, allowing the storms to develop along the different boundaries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level wave moved through the Great Lakes region and brought numerous storms to Central New York, making it one of the more active days in July. Strong instability and shear lead to a severe weather outbreak across the region. A few storms became tornadic. The most notable tornado was a high end EF-2 that went through Rome, NY and led to significant damage. Fortunately, no fatalities occurred with this storm. However, there was one fatality in Canastota when a man was trying to protect his old car while a storm with an EF-1 tornado was passing through. Multiple other storms had damaging wind gusts that lead to widespread damage across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree on Dinglehole Road.

A few of these storms because strong to severe producing some damage to trees and power lines, mainly across Saratoga and Washington counties. One of these trees fell onto a home near Cossayuna Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and large branches were downed on the east side of Cossayuna Lake, one of which fell onto a home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system (MCS) and an upper-level wave moved through the region triggering widespread showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours. These features moved into a high instability environment with sufficient shear in place and plenty of moisture, resulting in a ripe environment for a severe weather outbreak. Damaging wind gusts were the main concern though some storms also had tornadoes. Widespread tree and wire damage occurred across Central New York with two microbursts and two tornadoes surveyed making it one of the most active days of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down trees and wires on North Main Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex and an upper-level wave moved through the region triggering widespread showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours. These features produced a few slow moving thunderstorms near the Finger Lakes region which dropped 2-4 inches of heavy rain in less than two hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water ran over roadways and downhill into a residence at the intersection of Dresserville Rd. and Dumplin Hill Rd. Water nearly surrounded the structure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level wave moved through the Great Lakes region and brought numerous storms to Central New York, making it one of the more active days in July. Strong instability and shear lead to a severe weather outbreak across the region. A few storms became tornadic. The most notable tornado was a high end EF-2 that went through Rome, NY and led to significant damage. Fortunately, no fatalities occurred with this storm. However, there was one fatality in Canastota when a man was trying to protect his old car while a storm with an EF-1 tornado was passing through. Multiple other storms had damaging wind gusts that lead to widespread damage across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down powerlines on Gaskin Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second of a multi-day stretch of hot and humid conditions continued on June 19, 2024 across much of eastern New York outside of the Adirondacks and eastern Catskills. Heat index (or feels like temperatures) reached between 95 and 104 degrees across the region.||The heat and humidity also resulted in isolated to widely scattered showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon hours. A few storms became severe downing trees and wires.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved through the area during the mid afternoon of July 16, 2024. One of the storm cells moved through Canastota, NY producing a tornado touchdown between the Conrail railroad tracks and Barlow Street. The tornado was witnessed by town officials, residents and a video security camera on Canal Street as it moved to the east along Barlow and Railroad Streets. Severe damage occurred to multiple residences along Barlow Street with significant loss of roofs and roofing material as well as blowing out garage doors and windows. Two sheds were destroyed and multiple large diameter tree trunks and limbs were snapped. An 82 year old male |succumbed to injuries sustained while he was outside as the tornado crossed Main |Street. The tornado continued down Center Street causing further damage to trees, |roofs and a few power poles. The tornado veered slightly to the northeast crossing|the Old Erie Canal, causing additional tree damage and tearing a metal roof off of |a pole bar along Canal Road. It then weakened and dissipated before striking a solar |farm which sustained nearly no damage. The damage found along most of the tornado track was consistent with maximum wind speeds around 100 mph which is within the EF-1 category of the Enhanced Fujita scale.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms carried through the area with scattered damage during mid afternoon of July 16 2024. Strong rotation in one of the storms caused a high-end EF2 tornado to touch down between the Erie Canal and Muck Road in Oneida County New York. The tornado passed directly through Rome with heavy damage and estimated maximum wind speeds to 135 mph, before lifting just past Griffiss International Airport. Two churches sustained significant damage to their roofs and steeples. Two|large brick buildings sustained major damage or removal of roofs, with complete collapses of some walls. Cascading bricks next to these buildings crushed multiple vehicles. A vehicle was also flipped in a parking lot, one recreational vehicle was pushed over; another was forced from the side of a house out into a driveway, colliding with a parked car. Hundreds of large trees were snapped or uprooted, with resultant damage to adjoining properties. There was significant loss of roofs or roofing material from multiple residences, as well as blown out garage doors or windows. Some yards and even exterior walls were impaled by missiles in the form of branches or other objects. A B-52 on display near a park was shifted off its base but remained upright and undamaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary supported scattered thunderstorms across Central New York. Ahead of the front, an unstable environment triggered rounds of repeating, heavy rain producing thunderstorms that moved over the same areas. Rainfall totals of 2-3 inches fell in less than 3 hours which contributed to localized flash and urban flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of the Campus Plaza near Applebees and Wendys was trapping vehicles from exiting the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms passed through the area with scattered damage. An intense microburst occurred in a wooded higher elevation location of southwestern Schuyler County about 2 miles north-northwest of Monterey. In addition to dozens of uprooted trees up to 2 feet in diameter, there was a focused corridor of large trunk snaps, both hardwood and softwood. Other than a chicken coop which was crushed, damage though intense was limited to trees. The damage pattern was immediately intense at the initial microburst impact and then diverged while dissipating across Switzer Hill Road. Other minor tree damage could be found up and down the road outside of the main microburst corridor.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small tornado touched down near Daisy Hollow Road in Virgil, NY with mostly tree damage. A few homes sustained minor damage to their roofs. The tornado tracked east northeast along Babcock Hollow Road. The tornado passed near Greek Peak Resort and lifted near Route 11 just south of Cortlandville. It is unclear if the tornado was on the ground the entire path as there were a couple of inaccessible unsurveyed hills. Drone footage from NY Office of Emergency Management showed a cyclonic flow pattern in some trees along Route 11 right before it lifted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level wave moved through the Great Lakes region and brought numerous storms to Central New York, making it one of the more active days in July. Strong instability and shear lead to a severe weather outbreak across the region. A few storms became tornadic. The most notable tornado was a high end EF-2 that went through Rome, NY and led to significant damage. Fortunately, no fatalities occurred with this storm. However, there was one fatality in Canastota when a man was trying to protect his old car while a storm with an EF-1 tornado was passing through. Multiple other storms had damaging wind gusts that lead to widespread damage across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado descended out of a rotating severe thunderstorm at 319 PM EDT about 2 miles east of Taberg NY on Palmer Road initially with minor tree damage, and then more tree damage near the intersection of Mitchell and Point Rock Roads. The tornado|strengthened while tracking across Streun, Sulphur Springs, Harris, and Capron roads where a some structures were impacted. This included a mobile home that was shifted off of its blocks with much of its roof covering missing and lofted from Harris Road|towards Capron Road. Residents were in a vehicle that got lifted and moved slightly, but they were uninjured. Additional roof and garage damage occurred at other Harris Road homes. 2 trees fell on a residence and outbuilding on Capron Road, with significant roof damage. This was the area that experienced the most damage and widest footprint of downed trees, with estimated winds up to 95 mph, placing it firmly into the EF1 range. The tornado progressed over the northern end of Podunk Road, crossing Route 26 while tearing off half of a roof. The damage pattern became more chaotic along Beartown Road, though there were a dozen small to medium branches that impaled the ground as missiles in a backyard. The tornado ended just over the Town of Western line about a mile south of Beartown or 2 miles north of the Stokes Corner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary supported scattered thunderstorms across Central New York. Ahead of the front, an unstable environment triggered rounds of repeating, heavy rain producing thunderstorms that moved over the same areas. Rainfall totals of 2-3 inches fell in less than 3 hours which contributed to localized flash and urban flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A residence was flooded on Meadowlark Ln.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Similar to the day before, strong to severe thunderstorms developed along a frontal boundary draped across the forecast area, south of Lake Ontario. Storms also developed along and east of the Lake Erie lake breeze, and then tracked east-northeast to the Northern Finger Lakes and to Oswego County. Areas south of the frontal boundary had plentiful surface based CAPE values of over 2,000 J/kg, allowing the storms to develop along the different boundaries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires downed in the City of Oswego.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Similar to the day before, strong to severe thunderstorms developed along a frontal boundary draped across the forecast area, south of Lake Ontario. Storms also developed along and east of the Lake Erie lake breeze, and then tracked east-northeast to the Northern Finger Lakes and to Oswego County. Areas south of the frontal boundary had plentiful surface based CAPE values of over 2,000 J/kg, allowing the storms to develop along the different boundaries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree downed in the southbound lane on State Route 14 at Gifford Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system (MCS) and an upper-level wave moved through the region triggering widespread showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours. These features moved into a high instability environment with sufficient shear in place and plenty of moisture, resulting in a ripe environment for a severe weather outbreak. Damaging wind gusts were the main concern though some storms also had tornadoes. Widespread tree and wire damage occurred across Central New York with two microbursts and two tornadoes surveyed making it one of the most active days of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms quickly surged through in late afternoon with a bow echo radar signature to produce damaging winds for much of the area, but a higher end embedded EF1 tornado managed to spin up at a kink in the line where strong rotation was indicated on radar. The tornado took a meandering path through rugged terrain, with discontinuous damage at times from apparent skipping. The first tornadic damage began around Upham Road near Lane Hill in Lincklaen State Forest, and |crossed Hyer and Neal Roads as well. The tornado intensified to higher end EF1 when it reached an exposed ridge along Joe Road, with hundreds of uprooted and snapped large softwood trees in a swath over 200 yards wide. Tree snaps and uproots continued across County Road 13 and then somewhat intensified on the exposed higher elevation of Ridge Road while climbing into Bucks Brook State Forest, before finally dropping into the Otselic Valley to cause additional trunk snaps and uproots. A barn was partially|collapsed, with roof displaced, in Otselic Center. Other structures had minor roofing or window damage from Otselic Center, up Highway 26, to the tornado path ending along Route 80 in Otselic itself where a carport was also lofted. Other pockets of damage such as in Crumb Hill as well as towards South Otselic were more consistent with downbursts from the severe line of thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low pressure system was the driving force of scattered storms across Central New York. Warm, moist air became unstable enough for isolated severe storms. These storms had strong wind gusts that knocked over trees and wires in Tioga and Broome Counties. Small hail was also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree on Bunn Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low pressure system was the driving force of scattered storms across Central New York. Warm and moist air contributed to several rounds of heavy rain producing thunderstorms over the Central Southern Tier. Rainfall amounts in a short period of time totalled between 2 and 3 inches leading to localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rapidly flowing water was reported about to enter a residence at this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third of a multi-day stretch of hot and humid conditions continued on June 20, 2024 across much of eastern New York outside of the Adirondacks and eastern Catskills. Heat index (or feels like temperatures) reached between 95 and 104 degrees across the region. In anticipation of dangerous conditions, several school districts made the decision to move elementary and middle school students to a half day schedule on Thursday June 20, 2024 including East Greenbush, Bethlehem, Lansingburgh, North Colonie, Stillwater, Voorheesville, and Schenectady. Additionally, the governor of NY activated the Natonal Guard and the state Emergency Operations Center to aid in any emergencies that arose related to the heat.||A strong upper-level ridge began to sink south through eastern New York on Thursday, June 20, giving way to a surface trough tracking north to south. Several upper-level shortwave disturbances within the flow aloft worked in tandem with this surface feature to contribute forcing for ascent in an environment abundant with instability due to antecedent, higher-than-normal heat and humidity. The result was scattered showers and thunderstorms, some of which became severe in nature in the counties of Warren, Washington, Saratoga, Albany, Rensselaer, and Columbia. ||Severe thunderstorms resulted in numerous reports of tree and wire damage across the aforementioned areas in addition to nearly 48,000 people without power. The area greatest impacted was the city of Albany where the results of a storm survey concluded the occurrence of straight line winds with maximized speed of 75 mph. Here, substantial tree damage was sustained in Washington Park where numerous, hardwood tree branches fell and some trees were even uprooted. Additionally, isolated structural damage was also noted as a result of severe thunderstorms in the city of Albany. The Ten Broeck Mansion sustained significant tree damage, a steeple was partially blown off of St. Joseph's Church in the Ten Broeck Triangle Neighborhood, and a building on Thatcher Street partially collapsed resulting in its scheduled demolition (though it was not anticipated to be a total loss).

June 21, 2024 was the fourth day in the stretch of hot, humid conditions though, unlike its predecessors, only featured such conditions in the lower Mid-Hudson Valley. Heat index (or feels like temperatures) reached between 95 and 100 degrees across eastern Ulster and Dutchess Counties.||A lingering surface trough and weak cold front tracked southward through eastern New York on June 21. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed blocking Union Center Road in Ulster Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June 23, 2024 was the sixth and final day in the stretch of hot, humid conditions and, like the two days prior, only featured such conditions in the lower Mid-Hudson Valley. Heat index (or feels like temperatures) reached between 95 and 104 degrees across eastern Ulster and Dutchess Counties.||A low pressure system and potent upper shortwave tracked east from the Great Lakes region, lifting a warm front through eastern New York on the morning of June 23. With a cold front to the west, eastern New York fell into the warm sector of the system with warm, moist antecedent conditions contributing to sufficient instability. Higher values of low- and deep-layer shear and steep lapse rates paired with ample forcing from a low level jet gave way to scattered showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon of June 23. Some storms became severe, leading to numerous reports of tree and wire damage in Fulton, Montgomery, Washington, Rensselaer, Dutchess, Ulster, and Schenectady Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An emergency manager reported several instances of downed trees and wires in Fort Edward on State Street, Notre Dame Street, and Sullivan Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front tracking south east across eastern New York brought scattered showers and thunderstorms, some of which became severe in the Mid-Hudson Valley and Eastern Catskills. Severe thunderstorms resulted in numerous reports of downed trees and wires, some of which resulted in structural damage to a house and apartment building, in addition to nearly 5000 people without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed in Mt. Temper near NY-28.

Heat index (or feels like temperatures) reached between 95 and 100 degrees across eastern Ulster and Dutchess Counties.||A lingering surface trough and weak cold front tracked southward through eastern New York on June 21. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were downed along Mac Elroy Road near Jonesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June 23, 2024 was the sixth and final day in the stretch of hot, humid conditions and, like the two days prior, only featured such conditions in the lower Mid-Hudson Valley. Heat index (or feels like temperatures) reached between 95 and 104 degrees across eastern Ulster and Dutchess Counties.||A low pressure system and potent upper shortwave tracked east from the Great Lakes region, lifting a warm front through eastern New York on the morning of June 23. With a cold front to the west, eastern New York fell into the warm sector of the system with warm, moist antecedent conditions contributing to sufficient instability. Higher values of low- and deep-layer shear and steep lapse rates paired with ample forcing from a low level jet gave way to scattered showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon of June 23. Some storms became severe, leading to numerous reports of tree and wire damage in Fulton, Montgomery, Washington, Rensselaer, Dutchess, Ulster, and Schenectady Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed on wires with power outages on Montgomery Street in the Village of Fonda.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front tracking south east across eastern New York brought scattered showers and thunderstorms, some of which became severe in the Mid-Hudson Valley and Eastern Catskills. Severe thunderstorms resulted in numerous reports of downed trees and wires, some of which resulted in structural damage to a house and apartment building, in addition to nearly 5000 people without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed on Route 28.

Additionally, the governor of NY activated the Natonal Guard and the state Emergency Operations Center to aid in any emergencies that arose related to the heat.

Several upper-level shortwave disturbances within the flow aloft worked in tandem with this surface feature to contribute forcing for ascent in an environment abundant with instability due to antecedent, higher-than-normal heat and humidity. The result was scattered showers and thunderstorms, some of which became severe in nature in the counties of Warren, Washington, Saratoga, Albany, Rensselaer, and Columbia. ||Severe thunderstorms resulted in numerous reports of tree and wire damage across the aforementioned areas in addition to nearly 48,000 people without power. The area greatest impacted was the city of Albany where the results of a storm survey concluded the occurrence of straight line winds with maximized speed of 75 mph. Here, substantial tree damage was sustained in Washington Park where numerous, hardwood tree branches fell and some trees were even uprooted. Additionally, isolated structural damage was also noted as a result of severe thunderstorms in the city of Albany. The Ten Broeck Mansion sustained significant tree damage, a steeple was partially blown off of St. Joseph's Church in the Ten Broeck Triangle Neighborhood, and a building on Thatcher Street partially collapsed resulting in its scheduled demolition (though it was not anticipated to be a total loss).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were downed on South Main Avenue between Myrtle Avenue and Park Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third of a multi-day stretch of hot and humid conditions continued on June 20, 2024 across much of eastern New York outside of the Adirondacks and eastern Catskills. In anticipation of dangerous conditions, several school districts made the decision to move elementary and middle school students to a half day schedule on Thursday June 20, 2024 including East Greenbush, Bethlehem, Lansingburgh, North Colonie, Stillwater, Voorheesville, and Schenectady. The result was scattered showers and thunderstorms, some of which became severe in nature in the counties of Warren, Washington, Saratoga, Albany, Rensselaer, and Columbia. ||Severe thunderstorms resulted in numerous reports of tree and wire damage across the aforementioned areas in addition to nearly 48,000 people without power. The area greatest impacted was the city of Albany where the results of a storm survey concluded the occurrence of straight line winds with maximized speed of 75 mph. Here, substantial tree damage was sustained in Washington Park where numerous, hardwood tree branches fell and some trees were even uprooted. Additionally, isolated structural damage was also noted as a result of severe thunderstorms in the city of Albany. The Ten Broeck Mansion sustained significant tree damage, a steeple was partially blown off of St. Joseph's Church in the Ten Broeck Triangle Neighborhood, and a building on Thatcher Street partially collapsed resulting in its scheduled demolition (though it was not anticipated to be a total loss).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey concluded straight line wind damage occurred in the city of Albany on June 20, 2024. The majority of the damage occurred in the Washington Park area where numerous branches fell along with some uprooted, hardwood trees. All of the trees and branches fell in an easterly direction which is consistent with straight line winds. Isolated structural damage was also noted in the Ten Broeck Triangle neighborhood and near the intersection of Broadway and Thatcher Street. Maximum wind speeds were estimated to be around 75 mph. The path of damage was 1.55 miles long and 590 yards wide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June 21, 2024 was the fourth day in the stretch of hot, humid conditions though, unlike its predecessors, only featured such conditions in the lower Mid-Hudson Valley. Heat index (or feels like temperatures) reached between 95 and 100 degrees across eastern Ulster and Dutchess Counties.||A lingering surface trough and weak cold front tracked southward through eastern New York on June 21. Warm, moist antecedent conditions aided in providing a fairly unstable environment and requisite values of several severe weather parameters working in tandem with the aforementioned forcing mechanisms drove scattered severe thunderstorms in the Capital District and Mid-Hudson Valley.||Severe thunderstorms resulted in numerous reports of downed trees and wires in Ulster, Saratoga, Rensselaer, and Dutchess Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of wires were downed in the vicinity of North Troy and Brunswick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June 23, 2024 was the sixth and final day in the stretch of hot, humid conditions and, like the two days prior, only featured such conditions in the lower Mid-Hudson Valley. Heat index (or feels like temperatures) reached between 95 and 104 degrees across eastern Ulster and Dutchess Counties.||A low pressure system and potent upper shortwave tracked east from the Great Lakes region, lifting a warm front through eastern New York on the morning of June 23. With a cold front to the west, eastern New York fell into the warm sector of the system with warm, moist antecedent conditions contributing to sufficient instability. Higher values of low- and deep-layer shear and steep lapse rates paired with ample forcing from a low level jet gave way to scattered showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon of June 23. Some storms became severe, leading to numerous reports of tree and wire damage in Fulton, Montgomery, Washington, Rensselaer, Dutchess, Ulster, and Schenectady Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downed tree fell on a house near Argyle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed within a highly unstable atmosphere across most of western and north central New York. Thunderstorms were initiated by a weak lake breeze boundary across the area. Heavy rainfall from thunderstorms within a very moist atmosphere caused flooding issues as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total 24 hour rainfall from COOP observation station OLNN6 in Olean of 4.41 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed within a highly unstable atmosphere across most of western New York. Thunderstorms developed as an outflow boundary from upstream convection moved into western New York. Heavy rainfall within a very moist atmosphere caused some flooding issues as well during the morning of June 18.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amount measured since 5:00 AM EDT of 3.12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level low over the region provided cool temperatures aloft, along with marginal low level lapse rates and shear values. These parameters were just enough to develop some strong to severe thunderstorms over the Northern Finger Lakes as a trough axis crossed the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed in the south Canandaigua area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A convectively induced shortwave trough across southern Ontario province and eastern Lake Erie tracked across the forecast area. The trough, along with increased instability, caused strong to severe thunderstorms to develop east of the Lake Erie lake breeze. Severe Thunderstorm Warnings, along with reports of related damage were confined to portions of the Western Southern Tier, Upper Genesee Valley and the Northern Finger Lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees and wires downed in the Town of Leicester.

Heat index (or feels like temperatures) reached between 95 and 104 degrees across eastern Ulster and Dutchess Counties.||A low pressure system and potent upper shortwave tracked east from the Great Lakes region, lifting a warm front through eastern New York on the morning of June 23. With a cold front to the west, eastern New York fell into the warm sector of the system with warm, moist antecedent conditions contributing to sufficient instability. Higher values of low- and deep-layer shear and steep lapse rates paired with ample forcing from a low level jet gave way to scattered showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon of June 23. Some storms became severe, leading to numerous reports of tree and wire damage in Fulton, Montgomery, Washington, Rensselaer, Dutchess, Ulster, and Schenectady Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed across Route 5S west of Gordon Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A convectively induced shortwave trough across southern Ontario province and eastern Lake Erie tracked across the forecast area. The trough, along with increased instability, caused strong to severe thunderstorms to develop east of the Lake Erie lake breeze. Severe Thunderstorm Warnings, along with reports of related damage were confined to portions of the Western Southern Tier, Upper Genesee Valley and the Northern Finger Lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires downed on Route 243.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first of a multi-day stretch of hot and humid conditions began on June 18, 2024 across much of eastern New York outside of the Adirondacks and eastern Catskills. Heat index (or feels like temperatures) reached between 95 and 104 degrees across the region. In anticipation of dangerous conditions, several school districts made the decision to move elementary and middle school students to a half day schedule on Tuesday June 18, 2024 including East Greenbush, Bethlehem, Lansingburgh, North Colonie, Stillwater, Voorheesville, and Schenectady. Additionally, the governor of NY activated the Natonal Guard and the state Emergency Operations Center to aid in any emergencies that arose related to the heat.

A potent shortwave served as the primary forcing mechanism to drive showers and thunderstorms in the Mid-Hudson Valley on the evening of June 26. Some storms became severe in Dutchess County, resulting in numerous report of downed trees and power lines and nearly 17000 people losing power.  Additionally, an instance of a large tree downed onto a mobile home resulted in the death of an elderly man on Feller Court in LaGrange.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires were downed on Plass Road and Patricia Drive in Pleasant Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June 21, 2024 was the fourth day in the stretch of hot, humid conditions though, unlike its predecessors, only featured such conditions in the lower Mid-Hudson Valley. Heat index (or feels like temperatures) reached between 95 and 100 degrees across eastern Ulster and Dutchess Counties.||A lingering surface trough and weak cold front tracked southward through eastern New York on June 21. Warm, moist antecedent conditions aided in providing a fairly unstable environment and requisite values of several severe weather parameters working in tandem with the aforementioned forcing mechanisms drove scattered severe thunderstorms in the Capital District and Mid-Hudson Valley.||Severe thunderstorms resulted in numerous reports of downed trees and wires in Ulster, Saratoga, Rensselaer, and Dutchess Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed across Hoxie Road near Millbrook.

A cold front tracking south east across eastern New York brought scattered showers and thunderstorms, some of which became severe in the Mid-Hudson Valley and Eastern Catskills. Severe thunderstorms resulted in numerous reports of downed trees and wires, some of which resulted in structural damage to a house and apartment building, in addition to nearly 5000 people without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires downed on Route 214.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June 22, 2024 was the fifth day in the stretch of hot, humid conditions and, like the day prior, only featured such conditions in the lower Mid-Hudson Valley. Heat index (or feels like temperatures) reached between 95 and 102 degrees across eastern Ulster and Dutchess Counties.||A stationary boundary was draped across northern portions of eastern New York (southwest Adirondacks and Upper-Hudson Valley), creating a warm sector for areas to the south. Decent instability paired with requisite shear and steep low-level lapse rates allowed scattered showers and thunderstorms, some of which became severe, to develop. Severe storms primarily occurred in the Eastern Catskills and Mid-Hudson Valley, resulting in several reports of downed trees and wires. Additionally, an isolated thunderstorm in Herkimer County resulted in the occurrence of an EF1 tornado. The tornado touched down near West Winfield and followed along a path of 1.96 miles with a width of 200 yards and maximum estimated wind gusts of 100 mph. Damage consisted of detriment to a barn, destruction of a small shed and snapped and uprooted trees that fell and damaged two homes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near West Winfield, New York on Saturday morning, June 22, 2024. The tornado began near the intersection of Meeting House Road and Marriot Road and moved east-southeast ending east of New York State Route 51. The tornado damaged a barn and destroyed a small shed. The tornado snapped and uprooted trees and two houses were damaged by fallen trees. A metal canoe was wrapped around power lines. A video of the tornado was provided to the National Weather Service.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of showers and thunderstorms tracked across eastern New York during the afternoon hours on June 7, 2024. A few of these storms because strong to severe producing some damage to trees and power lines, mainly across Saratoga and Washington counties. One of these trees fell onto a home near Cossayuna Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires were downed on Fayville Road in the Town of Providence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front tracking south east across eastern New York brought scattered showers and thunderstorms, some of which became severe in the Mid-Hudson Valley and Eastern Catskills. Severe thunderstorms resulted in numerous reports of downed trees and wires, some of which resulted in structural damage to a house and apartment building, in addition to nearly 5000 people without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of wires down on Upper Frog Hollow Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and moisture rich storm system tracked across western New York on the night of Thursday April 11th and into the morning on Friday April 12th. Periods of steady and at times heavy rain fell across portions of the area, especially western New York. A Flood Warning was issued on the morning of the 12th for a large portion of the Niagara Frontier and points south to the western Southern Tier for excessive rainfall that had the potential to cause flooding in low-lying areas, urban areas and for small streams. ||Flooding was reported in Clarence Center on Lapp Road, resulting in a portion of the road being closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of Lapp Road in Clarence Center was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought periods of rain, some heavy, to eastern New York on April 12, 2024 resulting in some minor flooding along State Route 5 in Montgomery County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported along New York Route 5 between Mckinley Road near Palatine Bridge and Reservoir Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Miller Type B storm system (weakening low pressure to the west over the Great Lakes and strengthening low off the east coast) brought locally strong to damaging wind gusts, rain, and heavy, wet snow to eastern New York over the period of April 3-5, 2024. The greatest snowfall totals accumulated at the higher terrain regions of the Southern Adirondacks and Helderbergs where totals ranged from 7 to 12 inches with locally higher totals of 13 to 17 inches in the Southern Adirondacks. Elsewhere, totals ranging from less than 1 inch to 6 inches were common with the Mid-Hudson Valley generally accumulating the least amount of snow. ||This storm also produced strong to locally damaging wind gusts across eastern New York. While most recorded gusts generally ranged from about 30 to 45 mph, a few gusts ranging from 50 to 65 mph were recorded. An isolated, maximum gust of 85 mph was recorded in Rensselaer County which lead to the significant damage to a barn on a farm in the town of Petersburgh. ||The timing of some of the greatest snowfall rates intersecting the morning commute on April 4 made for hazardous conditions in many areas, leading to traffic delays. Additionally, the weight of heavy, wet snow paired with strong to locally damaging winds lead to numerous disruptions to traffic patterns and power outages from downed wires and trees. ||Key Impacts: travel delays, power outages, property damage, school closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An isolated 60 mph wind gust was measured by the Copake NY Mesonet site. There were multiple reports of trees and wires down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms tracked into western New York  as a shortwave trough and cold front crossed the area. A few of the thunderstorms became strong to severe. One storm developed a tornado in Allegany County. Heavy rainfall associated with the showers and storms did cause some Flash Flooding in Cattaraugus County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Buffalo Street closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent shortwave trough/Mesoscale Convective Vortex tracked southeast across the area, causing showers and thunderstorms for much of the Buffalo forecast area. A few of the thunderstorms became severe with tornado and wind damage, along with rain that was heavy enough to cause Flash Flooding issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car was stuck in water on Route 104 and North Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms fired up along a slow moving frontal boundary in an environment with 2000+ J/kg of SBCAPE and weak shear. Some storms became strong to severe and produced damaging wind gusts over southeastern NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed tree at Highway 306 and Pearl Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure slowly approached from the Mid-Atlantic coast and passed just south and east of Long Island, NY. A tight pressure gradient around the low pressure center helped produce high winds southeastern New York on April 3, 2024 through the early morning hours of April 4, 2024.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured high wind gust of 68 mph was recorded at Eatons Neck Lighthouse, NY at 1009 PM Local Standard Time at an elevation of 71 feet above ground. Additionally, a trained spotter reported that a 60 foot maple tree snapped in half near its root with multiple limbs down at 725 PM Local Standard Time in Stony Brook, NY.

Given 40 knots of deep-layer shear, some storms were able to organize and become severe. The storms produced damaging winds across southeast NY.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple instances of high winds were measured by mesonet stations from late in the morning through the evening. These include a sustained wind of 44 mph at 1034 AM and a wind gust of 65 mph at 1009 PM at Point O' Woods Yacht Club in Bayshore, NY, a sustained wind of 45 mph at 157 PM and a wind gust of 61 mph at 627 PM in Blue Point, NY, a 58 mph wind gust in Great South Bay near Amityville, NY at 318 PM, and a 61 mph wind gust at 259 PM at the Fire Island Campground on Fire Island, NY. All times listed are in Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers moved into the western New York area on the afternoon of Saturday September 21st. With shortwave trough over the area and within a northwest flow regime, along with around 1,500 J/kg of surface based CAPE thunderstorms developed within the showers. With conditions mostly favorable, thunderstorms became strong to severe at times during the afternoon. The thunderstorms caused damaging wind gusts and large hail to the size of golf balls. Heavy rainfall associated with the thunderstorms caused Flash Flooding in several areas across Allegany County. Thunderstorm wind damage was limited to only two counties, Allegany and Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree downed on Monahan Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep plume of tropical moisture had spread over Central New York ahead of the remnants of Tropical Storm Debby. Prolonged torrential rains blanketed the region during the night and early morning hours before becoming enhanced by the approaching tropical remnants. This enhancement of rainfall led to widespread flash flooding in many parts of the region where roads and bridges were destroyed and washed away along with numerous impacted properties. Several towns and villages experienced significant damage and destruction to homes and businesses, however the hardest hit area was in southern Steuben county along the Tuscarora Creek. The communities of Greenwood, Jasper and Woodhull were severely impacted with damage estimates totalling around 50 million dollars in this area alone. The Jasper-Troupsburg Central School was once again destroyed by floodwaters as it was back in a 2021 flash flood. Damages in Delaware county totaled around 10 million dollars and Broome county reported over $1 million in public infrastructure losses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two vehicles were reported to be trapped in floodwaters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Sunday October 6th, severe thunderstorms developed within the warm sector of a nearby passing area of low pressure. A pre-frontal trough along with increased instability and shear values caused thunderstorms to develop as the trough tracked into far western New York. As the trough and thunderstorms continued east from near Lake Erie, the increased instability over land, along with around 50 knots of bulk shear, caused the thunderstorms to expand in coverage and strength. Damaging thunderstorm winds and large hail were reported from interior portions of the Western Southern Tier, northeast to near Batavia and to near Rochester. Thunderstorms weakened as they tracked east into the Northern Finger Lakes and points east as instability and shear decreased.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees downed on power lines.

On August 9, 2024, remnants of Tropical Storm Debby impacted eastern New York, bringing periods of heavy rain and gusty winds. Total rainfall accumulations generally ranged from about 1-4 with several instances of flash flooding in the Capital District and isolated instances in the Eastern Catskills and Upper-Hudson Valley. Non-thunderstorm wind gusts throughout eastern New York peaked between about 35-50 mph, leading to numerous reports of tree and power line damage especially in Herkimer and Washington Counties. Nearly 25,000 power outages can also be attributed to strong winds or resulting downed trees and wires with Warren County being the most impacted with nearly 6400 people without power. Additionally, an EF0 tornado was confirmed by NWS Storm Survey in Ulster County. Hardwood trees were uprooted along the NYS Thruway near exit 18 as a result. ||Governor Kathy Hochul declared a State of Emergency in the afternoon of Friday, August 9th due to extensive flooding, thunderstorms and tornadoes across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local 911 call center reported the closure of Mattice Road in Prattsville due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep plume of tropical moisture had spread over Central New York ahead of the remnants of Tropical Storm Debby. Prolonged torrential rains blanketed the region during the night and early morning hours before becoming enhanced by the approaching tropical remnants. This enhancement of rainfall led to widespread flash flooding in many parts of the region where roads and bridges were destroyed and washed away along with numerous impacted properties. The Jasper-Troupsburg Central School was once again destroyed by floodwaters as it was back in a 2021 flash flood. Damages in Delaware county totaled around 10 million dollars and Broome county reported over $1 million in public infrastructure losses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A road and basement were reported to be flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Disturbances pivoted around an upper level low that was located over the Canadian Maritimes. Daytime heating combined with cold air aloft led to enough instability for spotty showers and thunderstorms to develop in the afternoon with one of these disturbances, mainly over the higher terrain. A couple of storms in Delaware and Sullivan Counties became severe producing locally damaging winds. A survey was completed in Sullivan County for a microburst that cause mainly tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst occurred in Roscoe, NY. The microburst went down a hillside and downed numerous trees in a uniform direction. Some additional damage from downed trees occurred on a few residential properties on Dutch Hill road. The microburst |ended before reaching Dutch Hill road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved along a stationary front postioned over northern Pennsylvania during the afternoon. Locally heavy rainfall accompanied the thunderstorms which triggered urban and flash flooding in the Hornell, NY area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were flooded in the city with water reported to be near homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On August 9, 2024, remnants of Tropical Storm Debby impacted eastern New York, bringing periods of heavy rain and gusty winds. Total rainfall accumulations generally ranged from about 1-4 with several instances of flash flooding in the Capital District and isolated instances in the Eastern Catskills and Upper-Hudson Valley. Non-thunderstorm wind gusts throughout eastern New York peaked between about 35-50 mph, leading to numerous reports of tree and power line damage especially in Herkimer and Washington Counties. Nearly 25,000 power outages can also be attributed to strong winds or resulting downed trees and wires with Warren County being the most impacted with nearly 6400 people without power. Additionally, an EF0 tornado was confirmed by NWS Storm Survey in Ulster County. Hardwood trees were uprooted along the NYS Thruway near exit 18 as a result. ||Governor Kathy Hochul declared a State of Emergency in the afternoon of Friday, August 9th due to extensive flooding, thunderstorms and tornadoes across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of downed trees and wires throughout Washington County due to strong winds. Many instances of downed trees and wires led to road blockages. Additionally, a couple of reports stated that trees had been downed onto residences.
