EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep plume of tropical moisture had spread over Central New York ahead of the remnants of Tropical Storm Debby. Prolonged torrential rains blanketed the region during the night and early morning hours before becoming enhanced by the approaching tropical remnants. This enhancement of rainfall led to widespread flash flooding in many parts of the region where roads and bridges were destroyed and washed away along with numerous impacted properties. Several towns and villages experienced significant damage and destruction to homes and businesses, however the hardest hit area was in southern Steuben county along the Tuscarora Creek. The communities of Greenwood, Jasper and Woodhull were severely impacted with damage estimates totalling around 50 million dollars in this area alone. The Jasper-Troupsburg Central School was once again destroyed by floodwaters as it was back in a 2021 flash flood. Damages in Delaware county totaled around 10 million dollars and Broome county reported over $1 million in public infrastructure losses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A bridge collapsed over Purdy Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex frontal system stalled over the Central Southern Tier of New York during the late afternoon and evening. This system triggered slow moving, heavy rain producing thunderstorms in the Elmira-Horseheads area which caused areas of flash flooding on area roads and small streams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Route 14 is flooded near the intersection of Philo Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Sunday October 6th, severe thunderstorms developed within the warm sector of a nearby passing area of low pressure. A pre-frontal trough along with increased instability and shear values caused thunderstorms to develop as the trough tracked into far western New York. As the trough and thunderstorms continued east from near Lake Erie, the increased instability over land, along with around 50 knots of bulk shear, caused the thunderstorms to expand in coverage and strength. Damaging thunderstorm winds and large hail were reported from interior portions of the Western Southern Tier, northeast to near Batavia and to near Rochester. Thunderstorms weakened as they tracked east into the Northern Finger Lakes and points east as instability and shear decreased.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot, humid conditions were present across eastern New York on Friday, August 2, 2024. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local 911 call center reported trees down in Pine Plains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Cyclone Debby tracked northeast across Central New York on Friday August 9. As the system tracked across the region, heavy rain pushed into parts of the forecast area, with the heaviest rain falling over the Southern Tier, Finger Lakes and up to the North Central New York areas. Flash Flooding was the primary threat from the remnants of the system, but some strong to severe thunderstorms also occurred over portions of the area.||Several reports of Flash Flooding were received from across the NWS Buffalo forecast area, with portions of the Southern Tier hit particularly hard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees down in Croghan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slowly dropping south across Lake Ontario stalled near/along the south shore of Lake Ontario. As the front approached the Western New York area, shear and instability increased. The front stalled a bit farther north than expected, causing more of the thunderstorm activity to develop over the Niagara Frontier. ||A tornado began at the shore of the Niagara River and tracked from near the south end of LaSalle Park into downtown Buffalo for approximately 1.4 miles. The tornado developed within a thunderstorm along converging lake boundaries from both Lake Erie and Lake Ontario. ||Heavy rainfall with some of the thunderstorms did result in Flash Flooding as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees either heavily damaged or completely downed in southern Akron. Metal roof of a pole barn on the same property slightly peeled back.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of TS Debby tracked through St. Lawrence County during the afternoon/early evening of August 9, 2024. This produced a 50-75 mile swath of excessive rainfall from 3 to 7 inches which led to widespread flash flooding. Subsequent information indicated over 100 roads damaged or washed out with several bridges damaged and/or destroyed. Some flood damage to homes also occurred in a few locations along with several swift water rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous flooding across St. Lawrence county as the results of heavy tropical rains (4 to 6+ inches) from Post Tropical Debby. In addition to flash flooding reports, there were numerous inundation, basement and other flooding.

Flash Flooding was the primary threat from the remnants of the system, but some strong to severe thunderstorms also occurred over portions of the area.||Several reports of Flash Flooding were received from across the NWS Buffalo forecast area, with portions of the Southern Tier hit particularly hard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few roads closed in the Town of Granby due to Flash Flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot, humid conditions were present across eastern New York on Friday, August 2, 2024. Heat index values (feels-like temperatures) reached the upper 80s to low 90s across much of the region, but mid to upper 90s were reached in eastern Ulster, Dutchess, and Washington counties, creating dangerously hot and humid conditions. ||Additionally, scattered thunderstorms, some of which reached severe strength in Dutchess County, developed across portions of the region in the afternoon and evening hours. Severe thunderstorms lead to several reports of tree damage in Dutchess County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air poured into the region on Wednesday December 11, allowing widespread rain to change to a little wet snow before ending. The airmass became marginally cold enough to support the start of a weak lake response by the evening of December 11. A secondary cold front then crossed the eastern Great Lakes overnight, bringing a much colder airmass into the area, coinciding with the start of a much more robust lake response. ||The heaviest lake effect snow fell Thursday through Thursday night December 12, when the coldest air and most favorable lake effect parameters aligned. Winds were strong enough, when combined with added forcing from the passage of the secondary cold front to allow for unusually strong inland extent of lake effect snow bands, with significant accumulations spreading far inland off both lakes.||Notable snowfall report amounts include: 42.5 inches in Eden, 41.2 inches in Hamburg, 41.8 inches in Worth, 33.0 inches in Barnes Corners, 28.0 inches in Copenhagen, 29.6 inches in Warsaw, 28.4 inches in Attica, 25.0 inches in Alexander, 17.5 inches in Dunkirk, 16.5 inches in Redfield and 9.3 inches in Perrysburg.

A slow moving cold front approached from the north at the surface while a well-defined shortwave trough approached aloft. With a very moist airmass in place ahead of the cold front, some thunderstorms that developed became severe and produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet site at the American Yacht Club measured a 61 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The pressure gradient tightened as a cold front approached from the west. This produced high winds over Southeastern New York early in the afternoon of December 11, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NY Department of Highways reported a downed tree on NY 27 eastbound in the area of Spring Close Highway in East Hampton at 127 PM Local Standard Time.

A favorable wind flow of cold air across Lake Ontario, due to a departing storm in eastern Quebec and high pressure across north-central Canada, allowed for a heavy lake effect snow event east of Lake Ontario. Snow band moved into portions of St. Lawrence and Franklin counties in New York before daybreak on December 12th and exited by the late evening hours. The result was a swath of 8 to 20+ inches across southeast St. Lawrence and southern Franklin counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were generally 8 to 20+ inches with 20 inches in Star Lake, 18 inches in Wanakena and 14 inches in Piercefield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged lake effect event developed over the Thanksgiving Weekend, caused by a blocking pattern and the first substantial cold airmass of the season. Temperatures aloft of around -10 degrees Celsius at 850 hPa over the lake waters of around 8 degrees Celsius, combined with passing shortwave troughs and abundant synoptic moisture provided a prime lake effect setup. Resulting equilibrium levels reached 15,000 feet east of both Lake Erie and Lake Ontario. Snowfall was measured in feet off of both lakes, with Lake Ontario having multiple reports of 5 feet or more, and multiple reports of 4 feet or more of snow were received off of Lake Erie.||Notable snowfall report amounts include: 65.9 inches in Copenhagen, 65.5 inches in Worth, 57.9 inches in Perrysburg, 56.5 inches in Cassadaga, 52.8 inches in Black River, 51.4 inches in Cattaraugus, 49.0 inches in Dewittville, 46.0 inches in New Bremen, 38.6 inches in Colden, 27.2 inches in Elma Center, 24.6 inches in Attica, 23.2 inches in Alden and 8.0 inches in Centerville.
The pressure gradient tightened as a cold front approached from the west. This produced high winds over Southeastern New York early in the afternoon of December 11, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured high wind gust of 60 mph was recorded at a mesonet station located in Stony Brook, NY (measurement taken at 159 feet above ground) at 614 PM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The pressure gradient tightened as a cold front approached from the west. This produced high winds over Southeastern New York early in the afternoon of December 11, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Department of Highways reported a downed tree at 147 PM Local Standard Time on Long Island Expressway/I-495 in westbound at Exit 34 in North Hills, NY with 1 right lane of 4 lanes closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry conditions across portions of eastern New York resulted in the development of severe drought conditions (Severe - D2) across portions of the mid-Hudson Valley starting on November 12, 2024 and continuing into December. On November 18, 2024, the New York State Department of Environmental Conservation upgraded region II to a drought warning which included the counties of Greene, Ulster, Schoharie and Dutchess, urging residents to reduce unnecessary water waste, while the rest of eastern New York was placed in a drought watch. The severe drought in the Mid-Hudson Valley, drought warning in region II, and drought watch elsewhere continued well into December before conditions improved. The drought warning was the first to be removed with region II (Greene, Ulster, Schoharie, and Dutchess Counties) returning to a drought watch by December 16. Said drought watch then remained in effect through the remainder of the month of December with D2 severe drought conditions also persisting in Greene, Ulster, Schoharie, and Dutchess Counties through the middle to end of the month.

An upper level trough slowly approached the area as a stalled frontal boundary sat just to our west. With an environment with about 1500 J/kg of MLCAPE and 30 kt of Effective Bulk Shear, thunderstorms that developed were able to become severe and produce damaging wind gusts and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed tree blocking NY 59 westbound at Deacon Smith Hill Road in Nyack.

An area of low pressure tracked from the Ohio Valley into southern Ontario and then into western Quebec. As the surface low tracked just west and north of the Buffalo forecast area, it also strengthened at the same time from 996 hPa to around 988 hPa. While the strengthening of the system wasn't overly impressive, it was enough when combined with the passage of its cold front and the cold air advection to bring winds of 55-63 mph across portions of western New York. ||Notable wind gusts include: 63 MPH at the Buffalo Airport ASOS and 59 MPH at the Niagara Falls Airport ASOS. Damage during this event included several reports of trees down across the warned area, including a few trees downed onto powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and powerlines downed in the Town of Pavilion.

Abnormally dry conditions across portions of eastern New York resulted in the development of severe drought conditions (Severe - D2) across portions of the mid-Hudson Valley starting on November 12, 2024 and continuing into December. On November 18, 2024, the New York State Department of Environmental Conservation upgraded region II to a drought warning which included the counties of Greene, Ulster, Schoharie and Dutchess, urging residents to reduce unnecessary water waste, while the rest of eastern New York was placed in a drought watch. The severe drought in the Mid-Hudson Valley, drought warning in region II, and drought watch elsewhere continued well into December before conditions improved. The drought warning was the first to be removed with region II (Greene, Ulster, Schoharie, and Dutchess Counties) returning to a drought watch by December 16. Said drought watch then remained in effect through the remainder of the month of December with D2 severe drought conditions also persisting in Greene, Ulster, Schoharie, and Dutchess Counties through the middle to end of the month.

Cold air poured into the region on Wednesday December 11, allowing widespread rain to change to a little wet snow before ending. The airmass became marginally cold enough to support the start of a weak lake response by the evening of December 11. A secondary cold front then crossed the eastern Great Lakes overnight, bringing a much colder airmass into the area, coinciding with the start of a much more robust lake response. ||The heaviest lake effect snow fell Thursday through Thursday night December 12, when the coldest air and most favorable lake effect parameters aligned. Winds were strong enough, when combined with added forcing from the passage of the secondary cold front to allow for unusually strong inland extent of lake effect snow bands, with significant accumulations spreading far inland off both lakes.||Notable snowfall report amounts include: 42.5 inches in Eden, 41.2 inches in Hamburg, 41.8 inches in Worth, 33.0 inches in Barnes Corners, 28.0 inches in Copenhagen, 29.6 inches in Warsaw, 28.4 inches in Attica, 25.0 inches in Alexander, 17.5 inches in Dunkirk, 16.5 inches in Redfield and 9.3 inches in Perrysburg.

An area of low pressure tracked from the Ohio Valley into southern Ontario and then into western Quebec. As the surface low tracked just west and north of the Buffalo forecast area, it also strengthened at the same time from 996 hPa to around 988 hPa. While the strengthening of the system wasn't overly impressive, it was enough when combined with the passage of its cold front and the cold air advection to bring winds of 55-63 mph across portions of western New York. ||Notable wind gusts include: 63 MPH at the Buffalo Airport ASOS and 59 MPH at the Niagara Falls Airport ASOS. Damage during this event included several reports of trees down across the warned area, including a few trees downed onto powerlines.

An upper level trough slowly approached the area as a stalled frontal boundary sat just to our west. With an environment with about 1500 J/kg of MLCAPE and 30 kt of Effective Bulk Shear, thunderstorms that developed were able to become severe and produce damaging wind gusts and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports 1 inch hail near Radcliff Drive in Upper Nyack NY.

A prolonged lake effect event developed over the Thanksgiving Weekend, caused by a blocking pattern and the first substantial cold airmass of the season. Temperatures aloft of around -10 degrees Celsius at 850 hPa over the lake waters of around 8 degrees Celsius, combined with passing shortwave troughs and abundant synoptic moisture provided a prime lake effect setup. Resulting equilibrium levels reached 15,000 feet east of both Lake Erie and Lake Ontario. Snowfall was measured in feet off of both lakes, with Lake Ontario having multiple reports of 5 feet or more, and multiple reports of 4 feet or more of snow were received off of Lake Erie.||Notable snowfall report amounts include: 65.9 inches in Copenhagen, 65.5 inches in Worth, 57.9 inches in Perrysburg, 56.5 inches in Cassadaga, 52.8 inches in Black River, 51.4 inches in Cattaraugus, 49.0 inches in Dewittville, 46.0 inches in New Bremen, 38.6 inches in Colden, 27.2 inches in Elma Center, 24.6 inches in Attica, 23.2 inches in Alden and 8.0 inches in Centerville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the period of December 4th through 8th, an area of low pressure tracking north of the Great Lakes caused widespread snow to track across most of the Buffalo forecast area early in the event, which was then followed by lake snow off of both Lake Erie & Ontario. East of Lake Ontario, the synoptic snow was enhanced by upsloping and then transitioned to lake enhancement and finally lake effect. East of Lake Erie, snow transitioned to mainly lake effect after a more noticeable break in the more widespread synoptic snow. The heaviest snowfall occurred east to southeast of both lakes, where amounts of up to 20 inches fell east of Lake Erie, and amounts up to 30 inches fell east of Lake Ontario. ||Notable snowfall amounts reported include: 31.3 inches in Highmarket, 30.0 inches in Lacona, 26.0 inches in Constableville, 21.6 inches in Cattaraugus, 20.9 inches in Springville, 20.1 inches in Colden, 19.0 inches in Pulaski, 18.1 inches in Attica, 17.5 inches in Little Valley, 16.9 inches in Warssaw, 14.0 inches in Stow and 12.2 inches in La Fargeville.

A deep upper level trough was over the eastern third of the US. Its axis approached through the day. Strong to severe storms developed well west of the area and eventually formed a broken line as they approached. While shear was minimal, a well-developed cold pool allowed for continued propagation with support for strong updrafts. The broken line produced an isolated damaging wind gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees down along the MTA Q line in Sheepshead Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough approached aloft as a cold front approached at the surface. Clusters of showers and thunderstorms developed west of the area. Given 0-6 km Bulk Shear and MLCAPE values around 1000 J/kg, one storm became severe and produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet site at City Island NY measured a 51 knot wind gust.

The pressure gradient tightened over the region as a cold front associated with the remnants of tropical system Debby approached from the west. This produced strong winds over southeastern New York during the afternoon hours of Aug 9, 2024.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported large tree limbs and branches down, occurring            between 1100 AM and 1200 PM Local Standard Time in Croton-on-Hudson, NY. One tree was one down on Rt 129 in the area of Dailey Drive with a road closure.

A negatively tilted upper level trough approached aloft while an associated surface low's triple point passed just north of the area. Severe thunderstorms formed along the cold front and produced damaging wind gusts the night of New Year's Eve into the early morning of New Year's Day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet site in Asharoken measured a 53 knot wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving Clipper system moved across NY and New England during the morning hours of December 24th. Snow began across northern NY during the mid to late evening hours of December 23rd and ended during the morning hours of December 24th.||A widespread 3 to 8 inches fell across the region with the lesser amounts in the Champlain Valley of Essex county and some higher amounts in St. Lawrence county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A general 4 to 7 inches of snow fell across southern St. Lawrence county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warmer than average temperatures during the previous three months along with insufficient rainfall, much below average streamflows, groundwater levels and dried out soil conditions continued areas of Severe Drought conditions in parts of East-Central New York state. These severe conditions lasted through the 10th before precipitation helped to alleviate drought conditions back into just abnormal dryness.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor declared Severe Drought over the eastern and southern portions of the county as a result of insufficient rainfall during the past 90 days coupled with low streamflows, groundwater levels and drier than average soil moisture.

These severe conditions lasted through the 10th before precipitation helped to alleviate drought conditions back into just abnormal dryness.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor declared Severe Drought over the eastern half of the county as a result of insufficient rainfall during the past 90 days coupled with low streamflows, groundwater levels and drier than average soil moisture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening area of low pressure moving southeast across Ontario approached the St. Lawrence Valley during the early morning of Thursday, the 5th. Ahead of this surface low, a strong push of warmer air led to snow showers developing during the afternoon and evening of Wednesday, the 4th, with upslope flow enhancing snow showers near the Tug Hill Plateau. As the surface low and strong supporting upper trough approached the area late Wednesday night, snow showers increased in coverage. As the cold front with this system then moved through prior to daybreak Thursday, locally heavy snow showers and snow squalls crossed the area from west to east. Northwesterly winds that developed in the wake of the front led to the development of lake effect snow showers which tapered off later Thursday night. Strong, gusty winds were in place through Thursday evening, leading to localized areas of blowing snow and reduced visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 6 and 14 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After about a twelve hour hiatus between steady snow events, a new prolonged period of lake effect snow developed downwind of Lake Ontario during the afternoon of Friday, the 6th, and continued through Saturday, the 7th, before tapering off later Saturday night. The snow came down heavy at times and primarily fell near and north of the NYS Thruway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 5 and 13 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak, upper-level disturbance led to widespread light to locally moderate snowfall across eastern New York on Friday, December 20, 2024. The heaviest snow fell within the Capital Region and northeast Catskills where three to six inches were common due to a period of moderate snow that appeared to be terrain-enhanced. Elsewhere, accumulations generally amounted to between one to three inches. Though this was not the first snowfall of the season, it certainly behaved as such, causing very slick road conditions especially throughout the Capital District and numerous accidents were reported via social media and from NWS employees during their commute into the office in the afternoon. ||Key Impacts: travel delays, vehicle accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 5.3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire, called the Whitehouse fire, started on November 8, 2024 in the Peekmoose Valley region in the Town of Denning before spreading rapidly up a steep ridge within the Sundown Wild Forest. The fire remained in forested area only destroying several outbuildings. The fire was contained on November 13, 2024 after burning about 645 acres. One firefighter was injured (direct) battling the blaze after suffering from chest pains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent clipper low pressure system impacted eastern New York late Wednesday, December 4 through Friday December 6, bringing widespread light to moderate and elevation-dependent, heavy snowfall; gusty winds; and lake effect snow. The heaviest snow fell in the Southwest Adirondacks and Eastern Catskills where upslope enhancement and lake effect snow bands contributed to moderate to heavy accumulations. Peak wind gusts throughout the region generally reached 35 to 45 mph with a peak gust of 48 mph in Ulster County. ||Key Impacts: travel delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 11 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a prolonged period of dry weather, a complex low pressure system impacted eastern New York from early November 21 through November 22, 2024. Over these two days, this storm brought region-wide rainfall and significant snowfall to the Eastern Catskills. Rainfall totals across most areas ranged from about 1 to 2.5 inches. Many areas also saw some snow, but accumulations generally fell below Advisory-level criteria outside of the Eastern Catskills. Within the highest terrain regions of Schoharie, western Greene, and western Ulster Counties, snowfall totals ranged anywhere from 7 to 16 inches with locally higher amounts maximizing at just over 18 inches. Here, liquid equivalents ranged anywhere from 2 to 3 inches with a local maximum of 3.67 inches associated with the maximum snowfall report of 18.6. The heavy, wet nature of the snow led to nearly 2000 power outages. Additionally, some schools were reported to have closed within the Mid-Hudson Valley area. ||Key impacts: power outages, travel delays, school closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 7 inches.||Precipitation in Schoharie County initially began as rain in the early morning hours of November 21, 2024 before gradually transitioning to snow that same evening. According to local snowfall reports, the western portion of Schoharie County, containing a portion of the Eastern Catskills with elevations reaching 1500 to nearly 3000 ft, initially saw the transition from rain to snow around 4 PM. However, accumulations didn't begin until around 9 PM. These areas subsequently saw the county's highest snowfall accumulations, ranging from about 7-12 inches with a local maximum report of 14 inches. Additional reports indicated that the eastern portion of Schoharie County, containing some higher terrain within the Helderbergs but overall lower elevations in comparison, didn't see a transition to snow until 9 PM with no accumulations until after 10 PM. Here, snowfall accumulations generally fell between 3-6 inches with an isolated maximum report of 8 inches at one of highest peaks of the Helderbergs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive upper / mid level trough lead to low pressure moving across the Ohio Valley with a secondary low developing just off the coast. Temperatures were warm and above freezing much of the time along the coast, but often at or below freezing for mainly the northwestern interior. This led to an advisory level snowfall across a good portion of Orange and Putnam counties.  Also, northern portions of Nassau County on Long Island received around 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cocorahs observers reported 4.8 inches in Warwick, 4.5 inches in Port Jervis, 4.4 inches at 4 SSE Chester, 4.0 inches at Greenwood Lake, 3.7 inches at Cornwall-on-Hudson, and 3.7 inches at 3.3 W New Windsor.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cocorahs observers reported 2.7 inches in Cold Spring and 2.5 inches at 0.3 S Nelsonville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An accumulating and advisory level snowfall occurred across northern portions of the Lower Hudson Valley in Southeastern New York. A departing strong high pressure system at 1048+mb and a weakening mid-level disturbance led to overrunning which resulted in a light accumulating snowfall. There was an expectation that less snow would be observed with less liquid precipitation overall and some freezing rain towards or just after precipitation onset. Thus, snowfall amounts in far northern sections was a bit more than anticipated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cocorahs observer reported 3.9 inches in Port Jervis, with trained spotters reporting 3.7 inches at 2 S Montgomery Airport and 2.5 inches at Monroe.

The heaviest snow fell in the Southwest Adirondacks and Eastern Catskills where upslope enhancement and lake effect snow bands contributed to moderate to heavy accumulations. Peak wind gusts throughout the region generally reached 35 to 45 mph with a peak gust of 48 mph in Ulster County. ||Key Impacts: travel delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 3.8 inches.

A weak, upper-level disturbance led to widespread light to locally moderate snowfall across eastern New York on Friday, December 20, 2024. The heaviest snow fell within the Capital Region and northeast Catskills where three to six inches were common due to a period of moderate snow that appeared to be terrain-enhanced. Elsewhere, accumulations generally amounted to between one to three inches. Though this was not the first snowfall of the season, it certainly behaved as such, causing very slick road conditions especially throughout the Capital District and numerous accidents were reported via social media and from NWS employees during their commute into the office in the afternoon. ||Key Impacts: travel delays, vehicle accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 4.4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level disturbance impacted eastern New York on Saturday, December 7 through Sunday, December 8 bringing light to moderate snow to much of the region. The heaviest accumulations fell across the Southwest Adirondacks courtesy of lake and upslope enhancement. ||Key Impacts: slippery travel conditions; travel delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 4.4 inches.

Showers moved into the western New York area on the afternoon of Saturday September 21st. With shortwave trough over the area and within a northwest flow regime, along with around 1,500 J/kg of surface based CAPE thunderstorms developed within the showers. With conditions mostly favorable, thunderstorms became strong to severe at times during the afternoon. The thunderstorms caused damaging wind gusts and large hail to the size of golf balls. Heavy rainfall associated with the thunderstorms caused Flash Flooding in several areas across Allegany County. Thunderstorm wind damage was limited to only two counties, Allegany and Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding on Voorhees Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms tracked into western New York  as a shortwave trough and cold front crossed the area. A few of the thunderstorms became strong to severe. One storm developed a tornado in Allegany County. Heavy rainfall associated with the showers and storms did cause some Flash Flooding in Cattaraugus County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees downed in Allegany County.

A prolonged lake effect event developed over the Thanksgiving Weekend, caused by a blocking pattern and the first substantial cold airmass of the season. Temperatures aloft of around -10 degrees Celsius at 850 hPa over the lake waters of around 8 degrees Celsius, combined with passing shortwave troughs and abundant synoptic moisture provided a prime lake effect setup. Resulting equilibrium levels reached 15,000 feet east of both Lake Erie and Lake Ontario. Snowfall was measured in feet off of both lakes, with Lake Ontario having multiple reports of 5 feet or more, and multiple reports of 4 feet or more of snow were received off of Lake Erie. ||Notable snowfall report amounts include: 65.9 inches in Copenhagen, 65.5 inches in Worth, 57.9 inches in Perrysburg, 56.5 inches in Cassadaga, 52.8 inches in Black River, 51.4 inches in Cattaraugus, 49.0 inches in Dewittville, 46.0 inches in New Bremen, 38.6 inches in Colden, 27.2 inches in Elma Center, 24.6 inches in Attica, 23.2 inches in Alden and 8.0 inches in Centerville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of November 20th, a strong occluded front tracked northeast across most of the Buffalo County Warning Area. Behind this front, strong winds increased out of the southwest to 45 to 55 MPH, especially over Lake Erie and areas near the lake. While the increase in wind only lasted about 4 hours, the sudden increase in the winds with the front, and the north to northeast direction of the winds prior to the front, caused a meteotsunami to develop on Lake Erie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The water level at Buffalo reached 9.175 feet at the NOAA/National Ocean Service gage located at the U.S. Coast Guard station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged lake effect event developed over the Thanksgiving Weekend, caused by a blocking pattern and the first substantial cold airmass of the season. Temperatures aloft of around -10 degrees Celsius at 850 hPa over the lake waters of around 8 degrees Celsius, combined with passing shortwave troughs and abundant synoptic moisture provided a prime lake effect setup. Resulting equilibrium levels reached 15,000 feet east of both Lake Erie and Lake Ontario. Snowfall was measured in feet off of both lakes, with Lake Ontario having multiple reports of 5 feet or more, and multiple reports of 4 feet or more of snow were received off of Lake Erie. ||Notable snowfall report amounts include: 65.9 inches in Copenhagen, 65.5 inches in Worth, 57.9 inches in Perrysburg, 56.5 inches in Cassadaga, 52.8 inches in Black River, 51.4 inches in Cattaraugus, 49.0 inches in Dewittville, 46.0 inches in New Bremen, 38.6 inches in Colden, 27.2 inches in Elma Center, 24.6 inches in Attica, 23.2 inches in Alden and 8.0 inches in Centerville.

A low pressure system tracking from the mid-Atlantic States to eastern New England brought a mix of rain and snow to eastern New York on Thanksgiving Day, November 28, 2024. ||Heavy snowfall set up across portions of the southeastern Adirondacks, central Mohawk Valley, Schoharie Valley and eastern Catskills where snowfall amounts between 7 and 14 inches were common. Snowfall amounts between 2 and 8 inches were common elsewhere except across eastern Ulster, Rensselaer, Columbia and Dutchess counties where snowfall amounts were around 2 inches or less, due to rain being the more dominant precipitation type. This snowfall resulted in snow covered and slippery roads impacting Thanksgiving travel.||A Pittsfield woman was killed (indirect) in a single car crash on Route 295 in the Town of Canaan after the driver lost control of the vehicle while driving in slippery conditions caused by a mix of rain and snow, according to local law enforcement.||Key impacts: travel delays, vehicle accidents.

Abnormally dry conditions across portions of eastern New York resulted in the development of severe drought conditions (Severe - D2) across portions of the mid-Hudson Valley starting on November 12, 2024 and continuing into December. On November 18, 2024, the New York State Department of Environmental Conservation upgraded region II to a drought warning which included the counties of Greene, Ulster, Schoharie and Dutchess, urging residents to reduce unnecessary water waste, while the rest of eastern New York was placed in a drought watch.

A multi-day lake-effect snow event occurred across northern Herkimer County and far western portions of Hamilton County from November 29 to December 2, 2024. The heaviest snows occurred along and north of Route 28, including in Old Forge and Inlet, and west of Routes 28N and 30 where 12 to 24 inches of snow fell.||Key impacts: Travel Delays.

Broad closed upper level area of low pressure across Ontario and Quebec provinces delivered a sustained WSW flow of cold air across relatively mild, even record warm lake temperatures beginning late November 28th through December 1st. This developed a prolonged single lake effect snow band that oscillated north and south during this period until it moved south of the region permanently on the afternoon of December 1st.||Lake effect snowfall totals of 3 to 4+ feet occurred in Jefferson county with southern portions of St. Lawrence county receiving  1 to 2 feet in some communities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across southeastern St. Lawrence county ranged from 6 to 20 inches with some specific totals including; 20 inches in Pitcairn, 18 inches in Balmat, 15 inches in Star Lake and 8 inches in Edwards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved across southern New England into the Gulf of Maine during the day of November 28th (Thanksgiving). Precipitation began shortly after daybreak with rain-snow mixed in the valleys and a wet snow in the higher terrain.||Snowfall totals in Essex county ranged from a few inches in the valley with up to 6+ inches in the highest peaks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals in Essex county ranged from a few inches in the valley with up to 6+ inches in the highest peaks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warmer than average temperatures during the previous three months along with insufficient rainfall, much below average streamflows, groundwater levels and dried out soil conditions prompted the development of Severe Drought conditions in parts of East-Central New York state. Conditions peaked during the second week of the month and held steady through the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor declared Severe Drought over the southeastern-most portion of the county as a result of insufficient rainfall during the past 90 days coupled with low streamflows, groundwater levels and drier than average soil moisture.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor declared Severe Drought over the eastern half of the county as a result of insufficient rainfall during the past 90 days coupled with low streamflows, groundwater levels and drier than average soil moisture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure that moved off the coast early on Thursday, the 21st, retrograded back to the north and west across the Catskills and into the Twin Tiers Thursday night into early Friday, the 22nd.  It tapped into moisture from both the Atlantic and off of Lake Ontario. Widespread rain showers spread from east to west as the low moved inland, and as colder air was drawn in from the northwest out of Canada, rain mixed with and changed over to snow especially late in the afternoon and evening on the 21st starting in the higher terrain and working down toward the valleys. Periods of snow continued overnight with some localized heavy banding across the western Catskills and Southern Tier where snowfall rates ranged from 1-3 inches per hour. As drier air moved in early on the 22nd, snow tapered off to some lingering rain and snow showers. With temperatures being marginally close to freezing during this event, the highest snowfall occurred in the higher terrain, especially above 1500 feet. Given the heavy, wet nature of the snow, there were scattered to widespread power outages with tree branches coming down due to the heavy load of the snow, especially across the southern Catskills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 3 and 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong disturbance dropped out of southern Ontario and across Central New York triggering the development of scattered showers and a few thunderstorms, mainly west of I-81. A couple of storms over Steuben County became strong to severe with reports of downed trees occurring on a couple of roads over central portions of the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds resulted in multiple reports of trees knocked down on both Turnpike Road and Hughes Road.

Showers moved into the western New York area on the afternoon of Saturday September 21st. With shortwave trough over the area and within a northwest flow regime, along with around 1,500 J/kg of surface based CAPE thunderstorms developed within the showers. With conditions mostly favorable, thunderstorms became strong to severe at times during the afternoon. The thunderstorms caused damaging wind gusts and large hail to the size of golf balls. Heavy rainfall associated with the thunderstorms caused Flash Flooding in several areas across Allegany County. Thunderstorm wind damage was limited to only two counties, Allegany and Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Deep water ponding on Dyke Street in Andover.

Widespread rain showers spread from east to west as the low moved inland, and as colder air was drawn in from the northwest out of Canada, rain mixed with and changed over to snow especially late in the afternoon and evening on the 21st starting in the higher terrain and working down toward the valleys. Periods of snow continued overnight with some localized heavy banding across the western Catskills and Southern Tier where snowfall rates ranged from 1-3 inches per hour. As drier air moved in early on the 22nd, snow tapered off to some lingering rain and snow showers. With temperatures being marginally close to freezing during this event, the highest snowfall occurred in the higher terrain, especially above 1500 feet. Given the heavy, wet nature of the snow, there were scattered to widespread power outages with tree branches coming down due to the heavy load of the snow, especially across the southern Catskills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 4 and 13 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance ejecting into the central Plains phased with a northern jet stream branch feature and a surface low formed over the Ohio Valley during the evening of  Wednesday, the 27th. The surface low tracked to the northeast and crossed over Southeast Pennsylvania Thursday, Thanksgiving morning, on the 28th and quickly reached southern New England by the afternoon. Precipitation started out as a mix of rain and snow late Wednesday night before changing over to snow in most areas and continued through Thursday morning. As the low tracked northward, a surge of warmer air aloft also pushed north causing a mix and even a changeover to plain rain at times along the Southern Tier and southern Catskills Thursday. With marginal temperatures in place, this event produced a heavy, wet snow which was elevation dependent. Scattered power outages did occur with the heavy weight of the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 2 and 4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers moved into the western New York area on the afternoon of Saturday September 21st. With shortwave trough over the area and within a northwest flow regime, along with around 1,500 J/kg of surface based CAPE thunderstorms developed within the showers. With conditions mostly favorable, thunderstorms became strong to severe at times during the afternoon. The thunderstorms caused damaging wind gusts and large hail to the size of golf balls. Heavy rainfall associated with the thunderstorms caused Flash Flooding in several areas across Allegany County. Thunderstorm wind damage was limited to only two counties, Allegany and Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portion of Traux Road washed out, Funland Skate Center inundated with waters, flooding on Trapping Brook Road and North Broad Street in Wellsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second of three days of hot and humid conditions continued across eastern New York on July 16, 2024. Heat index (feels-like temperatures) reached 95 to 104 degrees across much of the Mohawk and Hudson valleys.||During the afternoon hours, a strong upper level disturbance developed an organized line of thunderstorms which tracked eastward across the region. Widespread severe weather occurred along this line. Widespread and, in some areas, considerable wind damage was observed along with embedded tornadoes through the western and southern Adirondacks, Mohawk Valley, Upper Hudson Valley and Capital District with the most intense storms occurring along and north of Interstate 90. Observed wind gusts ranged between 50 and 87 mph with these storms with the latter wind gust reported at the New York State Mesonet at Glens Falls.||National Weather Service Storm Surveys, with some being assisted by other state authorities, concluded seven tornado touchdowns. Six of these tornadoes were rated EF-1 with estimated peak winds between 90 and 110 mph. These tornadoes occurred in the Town of Ohio (Herkimer County), Little Moose Lake/Limekiln Lake (Hamilton County), the West Branch of the Sacandaga River/West River Road (Hamilton County), Oxbow Lake (Hamilton County), Wilcox Lake Wild Forest (Hamilton/Warren County), and the Town of Day (Saratoga County). One EF-0 tornado touched down near The Glen in central Warren County with estimated peak winds of 85 mph. During clean up efforts from the Town of Day tornado, a woman was seriously injured (indirect) after being struck by a tree.||In addition to tornadoes, two microbursts, determined through remote damage assessment using drone footage and satellite imagery, were noted in Warren County. Both brought large areas of extensive tree damage with hundreds of hardwood trees being uprooted and snapped. The first was noted in western Warren County along and east of Stewart Creek with the second around Bear Point at the Tongue Mountain Range that extends into Lake George. ||These thunderstorms resulted in numerous to widespread power outages with Hamilton and Warren counties the hardest hit. Some power outages lasted for multiple days and some area roads were also impassable for days.||States of Emergency were declared for multiple municipalities including the City of Glens Falls and all of Warren and Saratoga counties. New York Governor Kathy Hochul also declared a state of emergency for all of New York State following all of the severe thunderstorms that impacted the state over the past week.||The governor requested a Federal Emergency Declaration following this severe weather event which included the counties of Hamilton, Herkimer, Montgomery, Saratoga and Warren counties.||Scattered thunderstorms gradually developed across the mid-Hudson Valley eastward across the central and southern Taconics producing some additional pockets of wind damage, but this activity was much less widespread and intense compared to the storms farther north.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey, with assistance from the New York State Police's Unmanned Aerial Systems program, was able to determine an EF1 tornado occurred along South Shore Road in the Town of Day. While much of the damage resulted from rear flank downdraft winds, drone footage indicated a narrow swath of tree damage consistent with low-level rotation. After coming ashore from Great Sacandaga Lake, the tornado proceeded over Bailey Hill producing additional tree damage to the east. The tornado then continued eastward producing a more concentrated area of damage along the northern shore of Hunt Lake before dissipating. The majority of the tree damage on the eastern shore of Great Sacandaga Lake was a result of strong rear flank downdraft winds. Estimated peak winds were 110 mph with a path length of 8.36 miles and a path width of 50 yards. During clean up efforts, a woman was seriously injured (indirect) after being struck by a tree.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey determined that an EF-0 tornado occurred across central Warren County. The tornado began along Route 28 just south of The Glen where numerous trees were snapped and uprooted and several utility poles were also snapped or downed. The tornado traveled to the northeast and produced additional tree damage along State Route 9 including Art Tennyson Road, Palermo Road, Tripp Lake Road and the Green Mansions development area. The tornado then veered to an east to east-northeasterly direction for the rest of its path crossing Interstate 87 near exit 24 producing pockets of additional tree damage. The tornado dissipated near Federal Hill Road. Estimated maximum winds were 85 mph with a path length of 9.64 miles and path width of 150 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep plume of tropical moisture had spread over Central New York ahead of the remnants of Tropical Storm Debby. Prolonged torrential rains blanketed the region during the night and early morning hours before becoming enhanced by the approaching tropical remnants. This enhancement of rainfall led to widespread flash flooding in many parts of the region where roads and bridges were destroyed and washed away along with numerous impacted properties. Several towns and villages experienced significant damage and destruction to homes and businesses, however the hardest hit area was in southern Steuben county along the Tuscarora Creek. The communities of Greenwood, Jasper and Woodhull were severely impacted with damage estimates totalling around 50 million dollars in this area alone. The Jasper-Troupsburg Central School was once again destroyed by floodwaters as it was back in a 2021 flash flood. Damages in Delaware county totaled around 10 million dollars and Broome county reported over $1 million in public infrastructure losses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Officials reported at least two residences were flooded on the first floor with several people trapped in the homes.

This enhancement of rainfall led to widespread flash flooding in many parts of the region where roads and bridges were destroyed and washed away along with numerous impacted properties. Several towns and villages experienced significant damage and destruction to homes and businesses, however the hardest hit area was in southern Steuben county along the Tuscarora Creek. The communities of Greenwood, Jasper and Woodhull were severely impacted with damage estimates totalling around 50 million dollars in this area alone. The Jasper-Troupsburg Central School was once again destroyed by floodwaters as it was back in a 2021 flash flood. Damages in Delaware county totaled around 10 million dollars and Broome county reported over $1 million in public infrastructure losses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread and massive flash flooding of Tuscarora Creek and surrounding tributaries devastated roads, homes and businesses between, and including, the villages of Woodhull and Addison. Homes were pushed off foundations and were seen floating downstream smashing into area bridges. Dozens of people were rescued from flooded homes and vehicles and displaced in temporary shelters for weeks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front in an environment favorable for severe weather with sufficient instability, plenty of moisture and wind shear. This supported the development of some strong to severe storms in the afternoon. Damaging wind gusts knocked down numerous trees and wires and there was one storm which produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down wires on North Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon and evening thunderstorms popped up in a very warm, moist and unstable environment. Weak perturbations riding along the edge of a strong ridge were enough to help spark the scattered thunderstorms near the Finger Lakes region. Damaging wind gusts were the main hazard with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down multiple trees on the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a cold front, and were most numerous over the Catskills and Sullivan County. A few storms became severe, producing damaging wind gusts that mainly knocked down trees and wires. However, one storm produced a severe microburst that cause damage at two separate campgrounds in Delaware County, with one injury and one fatality when a tree fell through a camper.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down power poles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary front, combined with moist air overrunning, it led to showers and thunderstorms during the early morning hours near the Mohawk Valley. One storm became severe producing large hail. Later in the morning into the afternoon and early evening, thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a shortwave trough and surface cold front. A few of the storms became severe producing damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple pictures and video taken of a funnel cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving cluster of thunderstorms developed along a boundary in a hot, humid and unstable environment. Isolated rain amounts in excess of 2.5 to 3.0 inches over a short period of time during the afternoon swelled local creeks and streams causing flooding in portions of Steuben and Cortland counties. A flooded road also occurred earlier in Seneca County, with initial activity the prior evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Floodwaters washed out Stratton Road over Sinclair Creek. There was significant damage to a culvert and bridge abutment.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree which blocked the road near the intersection of Sinclair and Turnpike Roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few weak disturbances tracked along the northern periphery of a strong upper ridge, triggering scattered thunderstorms. Many of the storms developed near a frontal boundary that was approaching the New York-Pennsylvania border. With heat, humidity and instability, some storms became severe producing damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down trees.

With heat, humidity and instability, some storms became severe producing damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree across Lattabrook Road which made it impassable from Sandys Lane to Burns Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact upper level disturbance moved across Lake Erie and into portions of the Finger Lakes region and into the Southern Tier of NY producing scattered thunderstorms late in the afternoon and early evening. The disturbance moved into an area with plenty of energy in the atmosphere, as well as capability to promote strong downdrafts. As a result, some storms produced locally damaging wind gusts across this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree on wires in a yard and on a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a cold front, and were most numerous over the Catskills and Sullivan County. A few storms became severe, producing damaging wind gusts that mainly knocked down trees and wires. However, one storm produced a severe microburst that cause damage at two separate campgrounds in Delaware County, with one injury and one fatality when a tree fell through a camper.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree on wires on Pennsylvania Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary slowly pushed southward over the region into a very warm, moist and unstable atmosphere. This boundary helped to initiate scattered thunderstorms early in the day, then storms became more numerous to the south where there was more instability and colliding outflow boundaries. Some storms became severe with damaging wind gusts, and in a couple cases, large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree and wires on a road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong microburst with estimated wind speeds of 60-85 mph winds, and an estimated peak wind of 95 mph, occurred between Groton/West Dryden and ended south-southeast of McLean on the evening of June 22nd. Multiple medium to large trees were observed being uprooted or snapped, as well as some tree damage resulting in property damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon and evening thunderstorms popped up in a very warm, moist and unstable environment. Weak perturbations riding along the edge of a strong ridge were enough to help spark the scattered thunderstorms near the Finger Lakes region. Damaging wind gusts were the main hazard with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree that took down wires on Havens Corners Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe microburst with estimated wind speeds of up to 90 mph, occurred just northwest to north of Cooks Falls, NY near the southeastern edge of Delaware County. There was sporadic damage spread across southern Delaware County from the severe thunderstorm that tracked through the area late in the morning. However, straight-line wind damage was most concentrated in a microburst footprint that encompassed Cooks Hollow and Butternut Grove in the Beaver Kill Valley, and especially up the Russell Brook drainage for at least a mile. The damage pattern diverged from the middle of the path, indicative of a downburst.||This severe microburst impacted two campgrounds with very damaging straight line winds. Many recreational vehicles and camper units, a few tents, and at least two vehicles were heavily damaged and in some cases destroyed by falling trees. Some of the trees or large branches exceeded a foot in diameter. A woman unfortunately perished when a large tree snapped and fell on her site, crushing it. At least one other person was injured with a hit to the head. There was an unknown number of additional |though minor injuries. Eyewitnesses reported that the winds came up suddenly and then lasted at least 5 minutes with one tree after another falling during that period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary front, combined with moist air overrunning, it led to showers and thunderstorms during the early morning hours near the Mohawk Valley. One storm became severe producing large hail. Later in the morning into the afternoon and early evening, thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a shortwave trough and surface cold front. A few of the storms became severe producing damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree on wires on York Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary front, combined with moist air overrunning, it led to showers and thunderstorms during the early morning hours near the Mohawk Valley. A slow-moving cluster of thunderstorms developed along a boundary in a hot, humid and unstable environment. Isolated rain amounts in excess of 2.5 to 3.0 inches over a short period of time during the afternoon swelled local creeks and streams causing flooding in portions of Steuben and Cortland counties. A flooded road also occurred earlier in Seneca County, with initial activity the prior evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was flooding portions of County Road 129 east of Romulus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary slowly pushed southward over the region into a very warm, moist and unstable atmosphere. This boundary helped to initiate scattered thunderstorms early in the day, then storms became more numerous to the south where there was more instability and colliding outflow boundaries. Some storms became severe with damaging wind gusts, and in a couple cases, large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong microburst with estimated wind speeds of 60-85 mph winds, and an estimated peak wind of 95 mph, occurred from the Tompkins and Cortland County border, near Sweetland Road and Nye Road, and ended north of Marathon on the evening of June 22nd. Multiple medium to large trees were observed being uprooted or snapped, as well as some tree damage resulting in property damage. A tree house collapsed due to multiple large trees snapping and falling, resulting in 1 reported injury. A small metal roof gazebo was also lofted 30 feet away from a house.
